test_id_1.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: The Provider Manual, effective October 1, 2025, offers more recent and specific guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims compared to the latest relevant Policy Update from December 1, 2024, and should receive more weight by default.
answers.policy_definition: The most recent policy update (December 1, 2024) generally advises practitioners to limit NYS Medicaid claim amounts to the actual invoice cost of the drug dosage administered and to refer to eMedNY provider manual web pages for additional program policy and billing guidance [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024]. Older policy guidance (August 1, 2021) specified that pharmacies could only bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members for immunization services, with dual eligible members continuing to access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021]. Other policy updates (April 1, 2023, and January 1, 2024) direct providers to specific eMedNY Provider Manuals for pharmacy claims guidance [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Update January 2024 Volume 40 Number 1:7, 8 — Jan 01, 2024].
answers.provider_manual_definition: For dual eligible pharmacy claims, the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made, and the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]. Per state regulation, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]. The NYS Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to ensure dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in accordance with federal and State laws and regulations [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — Oct 01, 2025]. All Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025]. Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments must determine if their business situation or business model requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both policy updates and provider manual guidance indicate that detailed instructions for pharmacy claims, including those potentially for dual eligible members, are primarily found within the eMedNY provider manuals [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024; New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Update January 2024 Volume 40 Number 1:7, 8 — Jan 01, 2024].
differences: The provider manual provides specific, detailed guidance on payment limitations, Medicare enrollment requirements, and claim submission obligations for dual eligible pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025], whereas the most recent policy updates primarily refer providers to the manual for such details [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024].

An older policy update (August 2021) explicitly stated that pharmacies could only bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members for immunization services, with dual eligible members accessing these services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021], a specific detail regarding immunization services not reiterated in the provided provider manual excerpts.
comparison: Both policy updates and provider manual guidance indicate that detailed instructions for pharmacy claims, including those potentially for dual eligible members, are primarily found within the eMedNY provider manuals [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024; New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Update January 2024 Volume 40 Number 1:7, 8 — Jan 01, 2024].

The provider manual provides specific, detailed guidance on payment limitations, Medicare enrollment requirements, and claim submission obligations for dual eligible pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025], whereas the most recent policy updates primarily refer providers to the manual for such details [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024].

An older policy update (August 2021) explicitly stated that pharmacies could only bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members for immunization services, with dual eligible members accessing these services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021], a specific detail regarding immunization services not reiterated in the provided provider manual excerpts.
answers.caveats: None

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
January 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Providers should refer to the NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy
Guidelines, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guide
lines.pdf, for further guidance on origin code and serial number values that must be submitted on the claim
for “pharmacy dispensing” when applicable for non-patient specific orders. Enter a value of “5” in field 419-
DJ (Prescription Origin Code) and a value of “99999999” in field 454- EK (Scheduled Prescription ID
Number). Additionally, providers should refer to the NYS Department of Health (DOH) Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Standard Companion Guide – Transaction Information, located at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
Reminder for Pharmacy and Medical Claims
The National Provider Identifier (NPI) of the ordering provider is required on the NYS Medicaid claim.
Additionally, providers must not bill for the provision of the RSV vaccine to NYS Medicaid members who are
also enrolled in Medicare.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2024 Volume 40 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Please note: The practitioner is expected to limit the NYS Medicaid claim amount to the actual invoice cost
of the drug dosage administered and maintain that documentation.
Practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages provided above, for
additional program policy and billing guidance. It is recommended that providers use electronic claims
submission whenever possible. These changes should ease practitioner burden by decreasing paper claim
submission and associated invoice documentation for claims with a DOS on or after October 1, 2024.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Pharmacy claims will initially be rejected for National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP)
Reject code "889" (Non-Matched Prescriber ID). This means the prescriber is not enrolled in NYS Medicaid.
• To override above rejection for the OOS prescription situations described above:
o In Field 439-E4 (Reason for Service Code): enter "PN" (Prescriber Consultation)
o In Field 441-E6 (Result of Service Code): enter applicable value ("1A", "1B", "1C", "1D", "1E", "1F",
"1G", "1H", "1J", "1K", "2A", "2B", "3A", "3B", "3C", "3D", "3E", "3F", "3G", "3H", "3J", "3K", "3M", "3N",
"4A")
o In Field 420-DK (Submission Clarification Code): enter "02" (Other Override)

Please note: The billing guidance above may not be used for claims or orders prescribed by an OOS licensed prescriber
that is treating more than one Medicaid member for more than a single instance of emergency care within a 180-day
period, or more than one Medicaid member for multiple instances of care when the services provided are more readily
available in another state within a 180-day period.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_1.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_1.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_1.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_2.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of 2025-10-01, offers more comprehensive and current guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims, including payment coordination and provider enrollment requirements, compared to the policy documents, whose most relevant specific guidance for dual eligibles is from 2021 and primarily addresses immunization services. Therefore, the provider manual should receive more weight by default for current guidance.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source dated 2024-02-01, pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims as outlined in the NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines and Medicaid Update articles [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01]. For general pharmacy claims, providers are directed to refer to the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy and the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01]. The policy documents state that for immunization services, pharmacies can only bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members, and dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, for dual eligible members, the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made, and the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Pharmacy providers of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. The NYS Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to ensure dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from enrolled providers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01]. All Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources implicitly acknowledge the existence of dual eligible members and their interaction with pharmacy services.

Both sources refer to the 'NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines' for further guidance on pharmacy claims, indicating a shared foundational document for pharmacy policy [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Update January 2024 Volume 40 Number 1:7, 8 — 2024-01-01].
differences: **Scope and Detail of Guidance:** The policy documents provide limited direct guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims, specifically stating that for immunization services, dual eligibles access through Medicare and pharmacies bill non-dual eligible members for Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01]. In contrast, the provider manual offers detailed guidance on payment coordination, provider enrollment requirements, and claim submission obligations for Medicare-covered services for dual eligibles [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01].

**Recency:** The provider manual's guidance on dual eligible claims is significantly more recent (Effective Date: 2025-10-01) and provides specific operational details [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01]. The most direct policy statement on dual eligibles is older (2021-08-01) and specific to immunizations [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01].

**Provider Obligations:** The provider manual explicitly states that Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services for dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01], and cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from these members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. The policy documents do not detail these specific obligations for dual eligible claims within the provided excerpts.
comparison: Both sources implicitly acknowledge the existence of dual eligible members and their interaction with pharmacy services.

Both sources refer to the 'NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines' for further guidance on pharmacy claims, indicating a shared foundational document for pharmacy policy [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Update January 2024 Volume 40 Number 1:7, 8 — 2024-01-01].

**Scope and Detail of Guidance:** The policy documents provide limited direct guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims, specifically stating that for immunization services, dual eligibles access through Medicare and pharmacies bill non-dual eligible members for Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01]. In contrast, the provider manual offers detailed guidance on payment coordination, provider enrollment requirements, and claim submission obligations for Medicare-covered services for dual eligibles [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01].

**Recency:** The provider manual's guidance on dual eligible claims is significantly more recent (Effective Date: 2025-10-01) and provides specific operational details [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01]. The most direct policy statement on dual eligibles is older (2021-08-01) and specific to immunizations [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01].

**Provider Obligations:** The provider manual explicitly states that Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services for dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01], and cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from these members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. The policy documents do not detail these specific obligations for dual eligible claims within the provided excerpts.
answers.caveats: The policy documents frequently refer to external manuals (including the Pharmacy Manual, which is the provider manual source) for comprehensive guidance, suggesting that the policy updates serve as announcements or clarifications rather than exhaustive policy definitions for all pharmacy claims. The specific policy guidance on dual eligibles for immunization services (from 2021) might be a carve-out or specific exception, while the provider manual provides broader guidance for general pharmacy claims for dual eligibles.

must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost. Providers should refer to the

www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, for requirements
when NYS Medicaid is secondary payor. Claim level identifiers are required on eligible 340B purchased drug
claims for NYS Medicaid members.
Through monitoring real-time claims, NYRx has identified two trends in pharmacy submitted 340B
drug claims:
1.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

The policy documents state that for immunization services, pharmacies can only bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members, and dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, for dual eligible members, the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. In contrast, the provider manual offers detailed guidance on payment coordination, provider enrollment requirements, and claim submission obligations for Medicare-covered services for dual eligibles [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01].", "**Recency:** The provider manual's guidance on dual eligible claims is significantly more recent (Effective Date: 2025-10-01) and provides specific operational details [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01]. The policy documents do not detail these specific obligations for dual eligible claims within the provided excerpts."], "caveats": "The policy documents frequently refer to external manuals (including the Pharmacy Manual, which is the provider manual source) for comprehensive guidance, suggesting that the policy updates serve as announcements or clarifications rather than exhaustive policy definitions for all pharmacy claims. The specific policy guidance on dual eligibles for immunization services (from 2021) might be a carve-out or specific exception, while the provider manual provides broader guidance for general pharmacy claims for dual eligibles."}
test_id_2.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_2.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_2.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_3.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, offers more recent and detailed guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims compared to the latest relevant policy document from February 1, 2024. Therefore, the provider manual should receive more weight by default for current guidance.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source dated February 1, 2024, pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims, as outlined in the NYRx Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines and Medicaid Update articles, submitting claims with identifiers and at actual acquisition cost when NYS Medicaid is primary payor [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01]. An earlier policy from February 1, 2023, states that Medicaid is the payer of last resort for dual eligible enrollees, requiring potential third-party reimbursement sources, including Medicare, to be billed prior to Medicaid [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]. For immunization services specifically, an August 1, 2021 policy indicates that dual eligible members access these services through Medicare, and pharmacies can only bill Medicaid for non-dual eligible members for immunizations [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source, effective October 1, 2025, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are federally required to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01]. The NYS Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies to ensure dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers in accordance with federal and State laws and regulations [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full, and pharmacy providers of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources imply that Medicare is the primary payer for dual eligible members, with Medicaid acting as a secondary payer or payer of last resort [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources indicate that providers must interact with Medicare for dual eligible claims, either by billing Medicare first or by submitting claims and accepting assignments for Medicare covered services [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources emphasize compliance with federal and state laws and regulations for pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly details payment limits and acceptance, stating that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee, no additional payment is made if Medicare is greater, and payment must be accepted in full without recovering Medicare Part B deductible/coinsurance from Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents state Medicaid is the payer of last resort but do not detail these specific payment rules.

The provider manual explicitly states that NYS Medicaid requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies to ensure dual eligible members receive services and that Medicaid enrolled pharmacies are federally required to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services for dual eligibles [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents refer to billing Medicare first but do not explicitly state a Medicare enrollment requirement for Medicaid pharmacies.

An older policy document (August 1, 2021) specifically states that dual eligible members access immunization services through Medicare and pharmacies can only bill Medicaid for non-dual eligible members for immunizations [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01]. The provided provider manual excerpts do not contain this specific carve-out for immunization services.

The newest policy document (February 1, 2024) mentions compliance requirements for 340B drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01]. The provided provider manual excerpts do not mention 340B claims.
comparison: Both sources imply that Medicare is the primary payer for dual eligible members, with Medicaid acting as a secondary payer or payer of last resort [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources indicate that providers must interact with Medicare for dual eligible claims, either by billing Medicare first or by submitting claims and accepting assignments for Medicare covered services [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources emphasize compliance with federal and state laws and regulations for pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly details payment limits and acceptance, stating that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee, no additional payment is made if Medicare is greater, and payment must be accepted in full without recovering Medicare Part B deductible/coinsurance from Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents state Medicaid is the payer of last resort but do not detail these specific payment rules.

The provider manual explicitly states that NYS Medicaid requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies to ensure dual eligible members receive services and that Medicaid enrolled pharmacies are federally required to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services for dual eligibles [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents refer to billing Medicare first but do not explicitly state a Medicare enrollment requirement for Medicaid pharmacies.

An older policy document (August 1, 2021) specifically states that dual eligible members access immunization services through Medicare and pharmacies can only bill Medicaid for non-dual eligible members for immunizations [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01]. The provided provider manual excerpts do not contain this specific carve-out for immunization services.

The newest policy document (February 1, 2024) mentions compliance requirements for 340B drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01]. The provided provider manual excerpts do not mention 340B claims.
answers.caveats: The policy documents contain a specific carve-out for immunization services for dual eligibles that is not present in the provided provider manual excerpts. Additionally, the newest policy document mentions 340B claims, which are not covered in the provided provider manual excerpts. While the provider manual is more recent overall, these specific areas might require consulting both sources or further clarification.

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims, as outlined in the NYRx
the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, and Medicaid
Update articles. All NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims (when NYS Medicaid is primary payor)
must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost. Providers should refer to the
NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, for requirements
when NYS Medicaid is secondary payor. Claim level identifiers are required on eligible 340B purchased drug
claims for NYS Medicaid members.
Through monitoring real-time claims, NYRx has identified two trends in pharmacy submitted 340B
drug claims:
1.

The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full, and pharmacy providers of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources imply that Medicare is the primary payer for dual eligible members, with Medicaid acting as a secondary payer or payer of last resort [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources indicate that providers must interact with Medicare for dual eligible claims, either by billing Medicare first or by submitting claims and accepting assignments for Medicare covered services [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources emphasize compliance with federal and state laws and regulations for pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly details payment limits and acceptance, stating that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee, no additional payment is made if Medicare is greater, and payment must be accepted in full without recovering Medicare Part B deductible/coinsurance from Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents state Medicaid is the payer of last resort but do not detail these specific payment rules.", "The provider manual explicitly states that NYS Medicaid requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies to ensure dual eligible members receive services and that Medicaid enrolled pharmacies are federally required to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services for dual eligibles [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents refer to billing Medicare first but do not explicitly state a Medicare enrollment requirement for Medicaid pharmacies.", "An older policy document (August 1, 2021) specifically states that dual eligible members access immunization services through Medicare and pharmacies can only bill Medicaid for non-dual eligible members for immunizations [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01]. The provided provider manual excerpts do not contain this specific carve-out for immunization services.", "The newest policy document (February 1, 2024) mentions compliance requirements for 340B drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01]. The provided provider manual excerpts do not mention 340B claims."], "caveats": "The policy documents contain a specific carve-out for immunization services for dual eligibles that is not present in the provided provider manual excerpts. Additionally, the newest policy document mentions 340B claims, which are not covered in the provided provider manual excerpts. While the provider manual is more recent overall, these specific areas might require consulting both sources or further clarification."}
test_id_3.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."

For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_3.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. test_id_4.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: The Provider Manual, effective 2025-10-01, offers the most recent and comprehensive guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims, while the policy documents either refer to the manual or provide older, specific instructions for immunization services. The Provider Manual should receive more weight by default due to its later effective date.
answers.policy_definition: For immunization services, pharmacies are only able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members, and dual eligible members will continue to access these services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01]. For general pharmacy claims, providers are directed to refer to the NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01; New York State Medicaid Update January 2024 Volume 40 Number 1:7, 8 — 2024-01-01] and other eMedNY Pharmacy Billing Guidelines [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01]. The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in accordance with federal and State laws and regulations [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Per state regulation, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources acknowledge the existence of dual eligible members in the context of pharmacy services.

Policy documents explicitly direct providers to the 'NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines' for comprehensive guidance on pharmacy claims, indicating the manual as a primary reference [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01; New York State Medicaid Update January 2024 Volume 40 Number 1:7, 8 — 2024-01-01].
differences: The provider manual, effective 2025-10-01, provides detailed requirements for dual eligible pharmacy claims, including the federal mandate for Medicaid-enrolled pharmacies to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare-covered services, payment limitations (not exceeding Medicaid-only patient amount), and the prohibition against recovering Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance from dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21, 22 — 2025-10-01].

Policy documents, particularly the most recent ones (2024-01-01, 2024-02-01), primarily refer providers to the Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines for general pharmacy claim guidance, rather than providing specific instructions for dual eligible claims within the policy updates themselves.

The only specific guidance on dual eligibles found in the policy documents is an older instruction (2021-08-01) stating that dual eligible members access immunization services through Medicare, and pharmacies can only bill Medicaid for non-dual eligible members for these services [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01].
comparison: Both sources acknowledge the existence of dual eligible members in the context of pharmacy services.

Policy documents explicitly direct providers to the 'NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines' for comprehensive guidance on pharmacy claims, indicating the manual as a primary reference [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01; New York State Medicaid Update January 2024 Volume 40 Number 1:7, 8 — 2024-01-01].

The provider manual, effective 2025-10-01, provides detailed requirements for dual eligible pharmacy claims, including the federal mandate for Medicaid-enrolled pharmacies to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare-covered services, payment limitations (not exceeding Medicaid-only patient amount), and the prohibition against recovering Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance from dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21, 22 — 2025-10-01].

Policy documents, particularly the most recent ones (2024-01-01, 2024-02-01), primarily refer providers to the Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines for general pharmacy claim guidance, rather than providing specific instructions for dual eligible claims within the policy updates themselves.

The only specific guidance on dual eligibles found in the policy documents is an older instruction (2021-08-01) stating that dual eligible members access immunization services through Medicare, and pharmacies can only bill Medicaid for non-dual eligible members for these services [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01].
answers.caveats: None

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims, as outlined in the NYRx
the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, and Medicaid
Update articles. All NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims (when NYS Medicaid is primary payor)
must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost. Providers should refer to the
NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, for requirements
when NYS Medicaid is secondary payor. Claim level identifiers are required on eligible 340B purchased drug
claims for NYS Medicaid members.
Through monitoring real-time claims, NYRx has identified two trends in pharmacy submitted 340B
drug claims:
1.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
January 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Providers should refer to the NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy
Guidelines, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guide
lines.pdf, for further guidance on origin code and serial number values that must be submitted on the claim
for “pharmacy dispensing” when applicable for non-patient specific orders. Enter a value of “5” in field 419-
DJ (Prescription Origin Code) and a value of “99999999” in field 454- EK (Scheduled Prescription ID
Number). Additionally, providers should refer to the NYS Department of Health (DOH) Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Standard Companion Guide – Transaction Information, located at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
Reminder for Pharmacy and Medical Claims
The National Provider Identifier (NPI) of the ordering provider is required on the NYS Medicaid claim.
Additionally, providers must not bill for the provision of the RSV vaccine to NYS Medicaid members who are
also enrolled in Medicare.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2024 Volume 40 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health
