test_id_5.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of 2025-10-01, provides more recent and detailed guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims compared to the latest policy updates (2024-12-01, which refers to manuals, or 2021-08-01 for specific dual-eligible guidance), and should therefore receive more weight for current guidance.
answers.policy_definition: According to the New York State Medicaid Update August 2021, "Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare" and "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members" for these services [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01]. More recent policy updates, such as the New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 and December 2024, generally direct providers to refer to eMedNY provider manuals for pharmacy claims and additional program policy and billing guidance [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01]. Similarly, for 340B drug claims, policy refers to the NYRx Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source, the New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, effective 2025-10-01, for dual eligible pharmacy claims, the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made, and the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Pharmacy providers of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Furthermore, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01]. The NYS Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies to ensure dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in accordance with federal and State laws and regulations [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources acknowledge the primary role of Medicare for certain services for dual eligible members, with policy stating dual eligibles access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01], and the provider manual detailing payment and enrollment requirements for Medicare-covered services for dual eligibles [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21, 22 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources implicitly or explicitly direct providers to comprehensive guidance, with policy documents frequently referring to provider manuals for detailed billing and policy guidance [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01], and the provider manual itself providing detailed policy [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — 2025-10-01].
differences: **Specificity and Scope:** Policy documents provide limited specific guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims, primarily noting that dual eligibles access immunization services through Medicare and that pharmacies bill for non-dual eligible members for these services [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01]. In contrast, the provider manual offers comprehensive and detailed guidance on payment limitations, acceptance of payment in full, prohibition of recovering Medicare Part B deductibles/coinsurance, and mandatory claim submission and Medicare enrollment for all Medicare-covered services for dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21, 22 — 2025-10-01].

**Recency and Authority:** The most specific policy guidance on dual eligibles is from August 2021, while the provider manual's guidance is more recent, effective October 2025, and is explicitly referred to by newer policy updates for detailed guidance [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01].
comparison: Both sources acknowledge the primary role of Medicare for certain services for dual eligible members, with policy stating dual eligibles access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01], and the provider manual detailing payment and enrollment requirements for Medicare-covered services for dual eligibles [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21, 22 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources implicitly or explicitly direct providers to comprehensive guidance, with policy documents frequently referring to provider manuals for detailed billing and policy guidance [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01], and the provider manual itself providing detailed policy [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — 2025-10-01].

**Specificity and Scope:** Policy documents provide limited specific guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims, primarily noting that dual eligibles access immunization services through Medicare and that pharmacies bill for non-dual eligible members for these services [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01]. In contrast, the provider manual offers comprehensive and detailed guidance on payment limitations, acceptance of payment in full, prohibition of recovering Medicare Part B deductibles/coinsurance, and mandatory claim submission and Medicare enrollment for all Medicare-covered services for dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21, 22 — 2025-10-01].

**Recency and Authority:** The most specific policy guidance on dual eligibles is from August 2021, while the provider manual's guidance is more recent, effective October 2025, and is explicitly referred to by newer policy updates for detailed guidance [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01].
answers.caveats: None

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.

Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

test_id_5.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_5.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_5.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_6.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_6.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document, effective April 1, 2025, and should receive more weight. Both sources define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims by establishing Medicaid as the payer of last resort, requiring other liable third parties, including Medicare, to be billed first.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy document, New York State (NYS) Medicaid is always the "payer of last resort" for pharmacy claims, and federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]. Providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]. Specifically for dual-eligible enrollees, "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment, and Medicaid regulations require providers to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. If there is a "responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. For dual-eligible patients, the manual indicates that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount for a Medicaid-only patient, and if Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Furthermore, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Providers are also responsible for determining if a patient is covered by other insurance and if that carrier covers the service [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23].
similarities: Both sources establish that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort" for pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources state that federal regulations require all other available resources or third parties to be billed and exhausted before Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].
differences: Policy documents explicitly name "Medicare" as a third-party reimbursement source that "must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" for "Dual Eligible Enrollees" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]. The provider manual, while discussing Medicare payments for dual-eligibles, uses the general term "responsible third party" for the primary payor in its newest policy guidelines [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual provides specific details regarding Medicare/Medicaid payment limits, stating that the total payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only rate and that Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance cannot be recovered from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. These specific payment rules are not detailed in the provided policy document chunks.
comparison: Both sources establish that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort" for pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources state that federal regulations require all other available resources or third parties to be billed and exhausted before Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Policy documents explicitly name "Medicare" as a third-party reimbursement source that "must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" for "Dual Eligible Enrollees" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]. The provider manual, while discussing Medicare payments for dual-eligibles, uses the general term "responsible third party" for the primary payor in its newest policy guidelines [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual provides specific details regarding Medicare/Medicaid payment limits, stating that the total payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only rate and that Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance cannot be recovered from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. These specific payment rules are not detailed in the provided policy document chunks.

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

Claims
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.

Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.

test_id_6.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
test_id_6.pm_ground_truth: For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
test_id_6.policy_ground_truth: This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.

test_id_7.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_7.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, is more recent than the policy documents (newest effective April 1, 2025) and provides more specific guidance for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, indicating Medicare as the primary payer. Both sources consistently define NYS Medicaid as the payer of last resort.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy guidance, New York State (NYS) Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, requiring providers to bill all applicable third parties liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as federal regulations mandate the use of all other available resources first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025]. It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, including commercial plans or Medicare, before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020]. Through coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims or up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — November 01, 2021].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual guidance, for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, Medicare is the primary payer. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made, and the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. Furthermore, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. Providers are responsible for determining if a patient is covered by other insurance (other than Medicare) and if that carrier covers the service being billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — July 23, 2025].
similarities: Both sources indicate that NYS Medicaid acts as the payer of last resort, requiring other liable third parties (including Medicare) to be billed first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025, by implication of Medicare being primary and Medicaid covering remaining responsibility].

Both sources imply that Medicare is a primary payer that must be billed before Medicaid for dual-eligible claims [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual explicitly details Medicare's primary role for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, stating that if Medicare payment exceeds the Medicaid fee, no additional payment is made, and providers cannot seek Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. Policy documents generally state Medicaid is the "payer of last resort" and covers patient responsibility after TPL/Medicare, without the same level of specific detail regarding Medicare payment exceeding Medicaid fees [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025] and [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — November 01, 2021].

The provider manual specifies that the combined Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the amount for a Medicaid-only patient [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025], a detail not explicitly found in the provided policy excerpts.
comparison: Both sources indicate that NYS Medicaid acts as the payer of last resort, requiring other liable third parties (including Medicare) to be billed first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025, by implication of Medicare being primary and Medicaid covering remaining responsibility].

Both sources imply that Medicare is a primary payer that must be billed before Medicaid for dual-eligible claims [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].

The provider manual explicitly details Medicare's primary role for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, stating that if Medicare payment exceeds the Medicaid fee, no additional payment is made, and providers cannot seek Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. Policy documents generally state Medicaid is the "payer of last resort" and covers patient responsibility after TPL/Medicare, without the same level of specific detail regarding Medicare payment exceeding Medicaid fees [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025] and [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — November 01, 2021].

The provider manual specifies that the combined Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the amount for a Medicaid-only patient [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025], a detail not explicitly found in the provided policy excerpts.
answers.caveats: None

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Through
coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable
claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being
covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare.
As previously communicated in the December 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled
Submission Guidance for Fee-for-Service Claims with Third Party Liability (Medicare or Other Insurance),
available at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no18_dec20
_pr.pdf, a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid with a zero-fill reimbursement from any TPL Plan must be
submitted. A pharmacy provider who receives a zero-fill response must ensure that the claim was adjudicated
within each TPL Plan claim process requirement. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate
coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System
(HCPCS) or by National Drug Code (NDC).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_8.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_8.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, provides the most recent and detailed guidance on primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, making it more current than the relevant policy document dated February 1, 2023. Both sources establish Medicare as the primary payer, with Medicaid as the payer of last resort.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest relevant policy document, Medicaid is the payer of last resort for dual-eligible enrollees, meaning all other reimbursement sources, including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]. It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, such as Medicare or Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual guidance, for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made, and the combined Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]. Furthermore, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]. Providers are also responsible for determining whether the patient is covered by other insurance and if that insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — Jul 23, 2025].
similarities: Both sources implicitly or explicitly establish Medicare as the primary payer for dual-eligible pharmacy claims. The policy states Medicare "must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023], and the provider manual discusses the combined Medicare/Medicaid payment structure, implying Medicare payment occurs first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources emphasize the provider's responsibility to identify and bill the appropriate primary payer. Policy states it's the pharmacy's responsibility to bill "to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020], and the provider manual states it's the provider's responsibility "to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — Jul 23, 2025].
differences: The policy documents explicitly define Medicaid as the "payer of last resort" for dual-eligible enrollees, requiring Medicare to be billed first. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023].

The provider manual details the payment limitations and rules once Medicare has paid, specifically stating that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only fee and that providers cannot seek Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources implicitly or explicitly establish Medicare as the primary payer for dual-eligible pharmacy claims. The policy states Medicare "must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023], and the provider manual discusses the combined Medicare/Medicaid payment structure, implying Medicare payment occurs first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources emphasize the provider's responsibility to identify and bill the appropriate primary payer. Policy states it's the pharmacy's responsibility to bill "to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020], and the provider manual states it's the provider's responsibility "to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — Jul 23, 2025].

The policy documents explicitly define Medicaid as the "payer of last resort" for dual-eligible enrollees, requiring Medicare to be billed first. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023].

The provider manual details the payment limitations and rules once Medicare has paid, specifically stating that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only fee and that providers cannot seek Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: None

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_8.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
test_id_8.pm_ground_truth: For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
test_id_8.policy_ground_truth: This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.

test_id_9.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_9.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more detailed guidance on primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, explicitly stating Medicare is primary and Medicaid is secondary, compared to the newest policy document, effective April 1, 2025, which broadly defines Medicaid as the payer of last resort. The provider manual should receive more weight by default as it is the more recent and detailed source for this specific issue.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy document, New York State (NYS) Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, requiring providers to bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid, as federal regulations mandate using all other available resources first. `[New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025]` For dual-eligible enrollees, specifically, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, meaning all other sources of reimbursement, including Medicare, must be exhausted and billed prior to billing Medicaid. `[Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 01, 2023]`
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, Medicare is the primary payer, and Medicaid acts as a secondary payer. The total combined Medicare and Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. `[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]` If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional Medicaid payment will be made, and the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. `[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]` Furthermore, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid recipients. `[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]`
similarities: Both sources establish that Medicaid is the payer of last resort for dual-eligible members. `[New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025]`, `[Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 01, 2023]`, `[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]`

Both sources indicate that other insurance, specifically Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid for dual-eligible claims. `[Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 01, 2023]`, `[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]`
differences: The provider manual provides specific details on the payment mechanism for dual-eligible claims, stating that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee, and no additional payment is made if Medicare covers more than the Medicaid fee. It also explicitly prohibits providers from recovering Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance from recipients. `[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]` Policy documents, while affirming Medicaid as the payer of last resort, do not provide this level of granular detail regarding payment caps or patient liability. `[New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025]`, `[Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 01, 2023]`

An older policy document (August 2021) states that pharmacies will "only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members" and that "Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare." `[New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 01, 2021]` This specific phrasing about *only* billing for non-dual eligible members is not present in the newer policy or provider manual chunks, which instead focus on Medicaid being the payer of last resort after Medicare, implying Medicaid *can* be billed secondarily for pharmacy claims.
comparison: Both sources establish that Medicaid is the payer of last resort for dual-eligible members. `[New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025]`, `[Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 01, 2023]`, `[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]`

Both sources indicate that other insurance, specifically Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid for dual-eligible claims. `[Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 01, 2023]`, `[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]`

The provider manual provides specific details on the payment mechanism for dual-eligible claims, stating that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee, and no additional payment is made if Medicare covers more than the Medicaid fee. It also explicitly prohibits providers from recovering Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance from recipients. `[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]` Policy documents, while affirming Medicaid as the payer of last resort, do not provide this level of granular detail regarding payment caps or patient liability. `[New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025]`, `[Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 01, 2023]`

An older policy document (August 2021) states that pharmacies will "only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members" and that "Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare." `[New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 01, 2021]` This specific phrasing about *only* billing for non-dual eligible members is not present in the newer policy or provider manual chunks, which instead focus on Medicaid being the payer of last resort after Medicare, implying Medicaid *can* be billed secondarily for pharmacy claims.
answers.caveats: An older policy document from August 2021 states that pharmacies can "only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members" and that "Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare." `[New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 01, 2021]` While this suggests Medicare is primary for immunizations and implies Medicaid cannot be billed for dual-eligibles for these services, newer policy (2023, 2025) and provider manual guidance (2025) generally describes Medicaid as the payer of last resort for dual-eligible pharmacy claims after Medicare, implying Medicaid *can* be billed secondarily. This might indicate a specific scope for the 2021 statement (e.g., only for immunization services) or a policy evolution, with the newer, more general guidance on "payer of last resort" being more broadly applicable to pharmacy claims.

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, provides the most recent and specific guidance on primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, superseding the April 1, 2025, policy update by explicitly detailing Medicare's role as primary for certain benefits and setting payment limits.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source dated April 1, 2025, New York State (NYS) Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, requiring providers to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. Federal regulations mandate that all other available resources be utilized before NYS Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]. Earlier policy guidance from November 1, 2021, clarifies that through coordination of benefits (COB), Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — 2021-11-01]. It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, which includes Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, for dual-eligible patients, the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made, and the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Per state regulation, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23].
similarities: Both sources establish that NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring providers to bill other liable third parties, including Medicare, before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01] (implied by Medicare/Medicaid payment rules and non-recovery of Part B costs).

Both emphasize the provider's responsibility to actively determine and bill other insurance carriers, including Medicare, for services rendered [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23].
differences: The provider manual provides more specific instructions for dual-eligible patients regarding Medicare Part B, explicitly stating that providers cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid, and that the combined Medicare/Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only fee [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents generally state Medicaid is the payer of last resort and may cover patient responsibility or excluded drugs, but do not detail the Part B deductible/coinsurance rule [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — 2021-11-01].

Policy documents explicitly state that Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being covered under the TPL Plan, including Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — 2021-11-01]. While the provider manual implies Medicaid's secondary role, its focus for dual-eligibles is more on payment limits and the non-recovery of Medicare Part B costs from Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both sources establish that NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring providers to bill other liable third parties, including Medicare, before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01] (implied by Medicare/Medicaid payment rules and non-recovery of Part B costs).

Both emphasize the provider's responsibility to actively determine and bill other insurance carriers, including Medicare, for services rendered [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23].

The provider manual provides more specific instructions for dual-eligible patients regarding Medicare Part B, explicitly stating that providers cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid, and that the combined Medicare/Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only fee [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents generally state Medicaid is the payer of last resort and may cover patient responsibility or excluded drugs, but do not detail the Part B deductible/coinsurance rule [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — 2021-11-01].

Policy documents explicitly state that Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being covered under the TPL Plan, including Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — 2021-11-01]. While the provider manual implies Medicaid's secondary role, its focus for dual-eligibles is more on payment limits and the non-recovery of Medicare Part B costs from Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].
