policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Through
coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable
claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being
covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare.
As previously communicated in the December 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled
Submission Guidance for Fee-for-Service Claims with Third Party Liability (Medicare or Other Insurance),
available at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no18_dec20
_pr.pdf, a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid with a zero-fill reimbursement from any TPL Plan must be
submitted. A pharmacy provider who receives a zero-fill response must ensure that the claim was adjudicated
within each TPL Plan claim process requirement. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate
coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System
(HCPCS) or by National Drug Code (NDC).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_10.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
test_id_10.pm_ground_truth: For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
test_id_10.policy_ground_truth: This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.

test_id_11.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more recent and specific guidance for pharmacy providers regarding co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members, stating they cannot recover Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance. This should receive more weight than the newest relevant policy guidance from May 1, 2024, which similarly prohibits providers of Medicare Part C benefits from recovering co-payments or co-insurance from dual-eligible individuals.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest relevant policy guidance effective May 1, 2024, a provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals, and is required to accept the Medicare payment [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024]. Generally, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, meaning all other sources of reimbursement, including Medicare, must be billed prior to Medicaid [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 01, 2023].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual guidance effective October 1, 2025, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicare and Medicaid dually eligible members, as the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. For Medicare Part D members, Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D plans, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — October 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources indicate that providers cannot recover certain cost-sharing amounts (co-payments, co-insurance, deductibles) directly from dual-eligible members for services covered by Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].

Both imply that the Medicare and Medicaid payments combined are considered payment in full for services rendered to dual-eligible members, with no additional recovery from the member [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].
differences: The policy guidance, effective May 1, 2024, specifically addresses providers of a *Medicare Part C benefit*, stating they cannot recover co-payment or co-insurance from dual-eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024].

The provider manual guidance, effective October 1, 2025, specifically addresses *pharmacy providers* of a *Medicare Part B benefit*, stating they cannot recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. It also provides specific rules for Medicare Part D members regarding drug coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — October 01, 2025].

The provider manual guidance is more recent (effective October 1, 2025) compared to the most relevant policy guidance (effective May 1, 2024) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024].
comparison: Both sources indicate that providers cannot recover certain cost-sharing amounts (co-payments, co-insurance, deductibles) directly from dual-eligible members for services covered by Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].

Both imply that the Medicare and Medicaid payments combined are considered payment in full for services rendered to dual-eligible members, with no additional recovery from the member [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].

The policy guidance, effective May 1, 2024, specifically addresses providers of a *Medicare Part C benefit*, stating they cannot recover co-payment or co-insurance from dual-eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024].

The provider manual guidance, effective October 1, 2025, specifically addresses *pharmacy providers* of a *Medicare Part B benefit*, stating they cannot recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. It also provides specific rules for Medicare Part D members regarding drug coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — October 01, 2025].

The provider manual guidance is more recent (effective October 1, 2025) compared to the most relevant policy guidance (effective May 1, 2024) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024].
answers.caveats: The provided documents offer specific guidance for certain types of providers (Medicare Part C providers in policy, pharmacy providers for Medicare Part B/D in manual) and benefit types. Neither source provides a comprehensive, general definition of co-payment responsibility for *all* dual-eligible members across *all* service types.

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their
usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible
amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.
Please note: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-
insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is
required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in
full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-
insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.
May 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.

The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Refusing to
provide services is an unacceptable practice.
o Providers may attempt to collect outstanding copayments through methods such as requesting the
copayment each time the member is provided with services or goods, sending bills or any other
legal means.
• Pharmacy providers must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the copayment that is
collected from a Medicaid member. Each claim that requires a co-payment will have the co-payment
automatically deducted from the final payment when the claim is approved for payment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

***Enrollment in this Medicare type is required when the pharmacy carries one or more items that would be required to be billed
to Medicare on behalf of a dual-eligible member.

test_id_11.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
test_id_11.pm_ground_truth: Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
test_id_11.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_12.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document, effective July 1, 2025, and should receive more weight for current guidance. Both sources generally agree that providers cannot recover certain Medicare co-payment or coinsurance amounts directly from dual-eligible members, though they specify different Medicare parts.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy guidance, effective July 1, 2025, dual-eligible individuals are subject to Medicare rules [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2, 3 — 2025-07-01]. A policy update from May 1, 2024, explicitly states that a provider of a Medicare Part C benefit "cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co- insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals" and is "required to accept the Medicare" payment [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01]. Medicaid generally functions as the payer of last resort, meaning it makes payments only after all other reimbursement sources, including Medicare, have been exhausted [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, states that "a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Me [...]" dual-eligible members, and that the "Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. For Medicare Part D members, it is generally "not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug" unless the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. While providers may attempt to collect outstanding general Medicaid copayments, pharmacy providers "must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the copayment that is collected from a Medicaid member" as the co-payment is automatically deducted [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources indicate that providers cannot recover certain Medicare co-payment or coinsurance amounts directly from dual-eligible individuals. The policy documents state that a provider of a Medicare Part C benefit "cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co- insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01]. The provider manual states that "a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Me [...]" dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources establish Medicare as the primary payer for services covered by Medicare for dual-eligible members. Policy documents state that "duals are subject to Medicare rules" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2, 3 — 2025-07-01] and Medicaid is the "payer of last resort" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]. The provider manual indicates it is generally "not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member" unless specifically excluded from Part D coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The specific Medicare parts addressed regarding non-recoverable co-payments/coinsurance differ: policy documents refer to "Medicare Part C benefit" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01], while the provider manual refers to "Medicare Part B benefit" and "Medicare Part D" for prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual includes general guidance on the collection of *Medicaid* co-payments, stating providers "may attempt to collect outstanding copayments" but "must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the copayment that is collected from a Medicaid member" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40 — 2025-10-01]. This specific guidance on general Medicaid co-payment collection methods is not present in the provided policy documents.
comparison: Both sources indicate that providers cannot recover certain Medicare co-payment or coinsurance amounts directly from dual-eligible individuals. The policy documents state that a provider of a Medicare Part C benefit "cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co- insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01]. The provider manual states that "a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Me [...]" dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources establish Medicare as the primary payer for services covered by Medicare for dual-eligible members. Policy documents state that "duals are subject to Medicare rules" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2, 3 — 2025-07-01] and Medicaid is the "payer of last resort" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]. The provider manual indicates it is generally "not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member" unless specifically excluded from Part D coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].

The specific Medicare parts addressed regarding non-recoverable co-payments/coinsurance differ: policy documents refer to "Medicare Part C benefit" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01], while the provider manual refers to "Medicare Part B benefit" and "Medicare Part D" for prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual includes general guidance on the collection of *Medicaid* co-payments, stating providers "may attempt to collect outstanding copayments" but "must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the copayment that is collected from a Medicaid member" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40 — 2025-10-01]. This specific guidance on general Medicaid co-payment collection methods is not present in the provided policy documents.
answers.caveats: The policy documents and provider manual documents focus on different parts of Medicare (Part C in policy, Part B and D in provider manual) when discussing the inability to recover costs from dual-eligible members. This suggests a complementary scope rather than a direct contradiction regarding which Medicare parts are covered by this rule. The provider manual's general guidance on collecting co-payments appears to apply to Medicaid co-payments, distinct from the Medicare co-payments for dual-eligible members that are explicitly stated as non-recoverable.

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their
usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible
amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.
Please note: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-
insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is
required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in
full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-
insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.
May 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Refusing to
provide services is an unacceptable practice.
o Providers may attempt to collect outstanding copayments through methods such as requesting the
copayment each time the member is provided with services or goods, sending bills or any other
legal means.
• Pharmacy providers must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the copayment that is
collected from a Medicaid member. Each claim that requires a co-payment will have the co-payment
automatically deducted from the final payment when the claim is approved for payment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_13.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, updated October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy update from July 1, 2025, and should receive more weight by default. Both sources consistently prohibit providers from recovering co-payment or co-insurance amounts directly from dual-eligible members, though they specify different Medicare parts and provider types.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest relevant policy, a provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01]. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring Medicare and other third-party reimbursement sources to be billed prior to Medicaid [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]. Providers must accurately report Medicare Part C cost-sharing amounts on Medicaid claims, implying Medicaid covers these amounts [New York State Medicaid Update October 2019 Volume 35 Number 10:5 — 2019-10-01]. The July 1, 2025 policy update emphasizes that dual-eligible individuals are subject to Medicare rules [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:3 — 2025-07-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. The Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. For Medicare Part D members, Medicaid generally does not pay for prescription drugs unless the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D coverage, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources prohibit providers from attempting to recover co-payment or co-insurance amounts directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources indicate that the combined Medicare/Medicaid payment should be accepted as payment in full, preventing further collection from the dual-eligible. The provider manual explicitly states, "The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources align with the principle of Medicaid as the payer of last resort, requiring Medicare to be billed first [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] and indicating Medicare is primary for drug costs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The policy guidance specifically prohibits providers of a "Medicare Part C benefit" from recovering co-payments or co-insurance from dual-eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01], whereas the provider manual specifically prohibits "a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit" from seeking to recover deductible or coinsurance amounts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

The policy guidance is general for "providers" of Medicare Part C benefits, while the provider manual's specific prohibition is directed at "pharmacy providers" for Medicare Part B benefits and also details Medicaid's limited role in covering Part D prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual (effective October 1, 2025) is more recent and provides specific guidance for pharmacy providers regarding Part B and Part D, while the newest policy update (effective July 1, 2025) broadly states that duals are subject to Medicare rules without specific co-payment collection prohibitions, relying on an older policy update (May 1, 2024) for that detail.
comparison: Both sources prohibit providers from attempting to recover co-payment or co-insurance amounts directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources indicate that the combined Medicare/Medicaid payment should be accepted as payment in full, preventing further collection from the dual-eligible. The provider manual explicitly states, "The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources align with the principle of Medicaid as the payer of last resort, requiring Medicare to be billed first [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] and indicating Medicare is primary for drug costs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

The policy guidance specifically prohibits providers of a "Medicare Part C benefit" from recovering co-payments or co-insurance from dual-eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01], whereas the provider manual specifically prohibits "a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit" from seeking to recover deductible or coinsurance amounts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

The policy guidance is general for "providers" of Medicare Part C benefits, while the provider manual's specific prohibition is directed at "pharmacy providers" for Medicare Part B benefits and also details Medicaid's limited role in covering Part D prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual (effective October 1, 2025) is more recent and provides specific guidance for pharmacy providers regarding Part B and Part D, while the newest policy update (effective July 1, 2025) broadly states that duals are subject to Medicare rules without specific co-payment collection prohibitions, relying on an older policy update (May 1, 2024) for that detail.
answers.caveats: The policy documents discuss Medicare Part C and general billing, while the provider manual focuses on pharmacy services and Medicare Part B/D. While both prohibit direct collection from dual-eligibles, the specific Medicare parts and provider types mentioned differ. The provider manual is more recent and offers specific guidance for pharmacy services.

https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement

• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
October 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

Policy and Billing
Reminder to all Providers on Billing Requirements for
Medicare Part C Claims That are Submitted to Medicaid for
Payment of Patient Responsibility
Reminder: All providers must accurately report Medicare Part C cost-sharing amounts on Medicaid
claims. Some providers may be inflating the patient responsibility amount for Part C Medicare Advantage claims,
resulting in overpayment by the Medicaid program and therefore may be leaving the providers subject to
investigation by the Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG).
A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot seek to recover any copayment, or coinsurance amount
from Medicare/Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is required to accept the Medicare Part C
health plan payment and any Medicaid payment as payment in full for the service. The member may not be billed
for any Medicare Part C copayment/coinsurance amount that was not reimbursed by Medicaid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2019 Volume 35 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_13.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
test_id_13.pm_ground_truth: Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
test_id_13.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_14.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest relevant policy guidance from July 1, 2025, and should receive more weight by default for current guidance. Both sources generally prohibit providers from recovering co-payments or co-insurance directly from dual-eligible members, but they differ in the specific Medicare parts and provider types addressed.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest relevant policy guidance, dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:3 — July 01, 2025]. For Medicare Part C benefits, providers cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024]. Providers are required to accept the Medicare payment and must submit claims using the appropriate format, indicating their usual fee, and reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible, co-insurance, co-payment, and paid amounts [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024]. Medicaid acts as the payer of last resort, meaning Medicare and other third-party reimbursement sources must be billed prior to Medicaid [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 01, 2023]. Providers must accurately report Medicare Part C cost-sharing amounts on Medicaid claims to prevent overpayment [New York State Medicaid Update October 2019 Volume 35 Number 10:5 — October 01, 2019].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual guidance, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. The combined Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. For Medicare Part D members, Medicaid only pays for prescription drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D plans, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources indicate that providers cannot recover co-payment or co-insurance amounts directly from dual-eligible individuals for certain Medicare benefits, with Policy stating this for Medicare Part C [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024] and the Provider Manual for Medicare Part B for pharmacy providers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources imply that the combined Medicare and Medicaid payment should be accepted as payment in full, with the Provider Manual explicitly stating it for pharmacy providers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025] and Policy stating providers are "required to accept the Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024] and must accurately report cost-sharing [New York State Medicaid Update October 2019 Volume 35 Number 10:5 — October 01, 2019].

Both acknowledge that dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules, with Policy stating "duals are subject to Medicare rules" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:3 — July 01, 2025] and the Provider Manual detailing specific interactions with Medicare Part B and Part D [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].
differences: The Policy documents primarily address co-payment responsibility for Medicare Part C benefits [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024], while the Provider Manual focuses on pharmacy providers and Medicare Part B and Part D benefits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].

The Provider Manual's guidance on co-payment recovery is explicitly for "a pharmacy provider" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025], whereas the Policy guidance applies more broadly to "the provider" of a Medicare Part C benefit [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024].

Policy explicitly states Medicaid is the payer of last resort and Medicare must be billed first [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 01, 2023]. While the Provider Manual implies this by discussing Medicaid's role after Medicare Part D, it does not explicitly state "payer of last resort" in the provided chunks.

Policy provides specific details on the provider's responsibility to submit claims with usual fees and report Medicare Advantage Plan deductible, co-insurance, co-payment, and paid amounts [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024], and to accurately report Part C cost-sharing [New York State Medicaid Update October 2019 Volume 35 Number 10:5 — October 01, 2019]. The Provider Manual focuses on what Medicaid will pay for Part D drugs and accepting payment in full for Part B [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources indicate that providers cannot recover co-payment or co-insurance amounts directly from dual-eligible individuals for certain Medicare benefits, with Policy stating this for Medicare Part C [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024] and the Provider Manual for Medicare Part B for pharmacy providers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources imply that the combined Medicare and Medicaid payment should be accepted as payment in full, with the Provider Manual explicitly stating it for pharmacy providers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025] and Policy stating providers are "required to accept the Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024] and must accurately report cost-sharing [New York State Medicaid Update October 2019 Volume 35 Number 10:5 — October 01, 2019].

Both acknowledge that dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules, with Policy stating "duals are subject to Medicare rules" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:3 — July 01, 2025] and the Provider Manual detailing specific interactions with Medicare Part B and Part D [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].

The Policy documents primarily address co-payment responsibility for Medicare Part C benefits [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024], while the Provider Manual focuses on pharmacy providers and Medicare Part B and Part D benefits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].

The Provider Manual's guidance on co-payment recovery is explicitly for "a pharmacy provider" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025], whereas the Policy guidance applies more broadly to "the provider" of a Medicare Part C benefit [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024].

Policy explicitly states Medicaid is the payer of last resort and Medicare must be billed first [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 01, 2023]. While the Provider Manual implies this by discussing Medicaid's role after Medicare Part D, it does not explicitly state "payer of last resort" in the provided chunks.

Policy provides specific details on the provider's responsibility to submit claims with usual fees and report Medicare Advantage Plan deductible, co-insurance, co-payment, and paid amounts [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024], and to accurately report Part C cost-sharing [New York State Medicaid Update October 2019 Volume 35 Number 10:5 — October 01, 2019]. The Provider Manual focuses on what Medicaid will pay for Part D drugs and accepting payment in full for Part B [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The provider manual chunks are specific to pharmacy services and Medicare Part B/D, while policy chunks cover Medicare Part C more broadly. This indicates a difference in scope rather than a direct contradiction. The newest policy chunk (2025-07-01) provides a general statement about duals being subject to Medicare rules, but the most detailed policy guidance on co-payment recovery for duals is from 2024-05-01, specifically for Medicare Part C.

policy.text:
• It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their
usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible
amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.
Please note: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-
insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is
required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in
full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-
insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.
May 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.

The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medical and Surgical Supplies
Reimbursement for each covered medical/surgical supply will be the lower of:
• The price as indicated on the New York State List of Medical/Surgical Supplies, or
• The usual and customary price charged to the general public.
"Covered supplies" are those on the OTC and Supply Fee Schedule manual located here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/index.aspx. For supplies not on this list, only those supplies for
which the prescriber has obtained prior approval are covered.
Co-payments for Drugs and Medical Supplies
The New York State Medicaid Program charges co-payments for many drug and medical supply items.
Health care providers have an obligation to provide services and goods regardless of a Medicaid member's ability to
pay co-payments:
• Pharmacy providers may not refuse services to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford to
pay the co-payment, per Social Security Act (SSA) § 1916 and Title 18 NYCRR § 360-7.12.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

The Provider Manual focuses on what Medicaid will pay for Part D drugs and accepting payment in full for Part B [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]."], "caveats": "The provider manual chunks are specific to pharmacy services and Medicare Part B/D, while policy chunks cover Medicare Part C more broadly. This indicates a difference in scope rather than a direct contradiction. The newest policy chunk (2025-07-01) provides a general statement about duals being subject to Medicare rules, but the most detailed policy guidance on co-payment recovery for duals is from 2024-05-01, specifically for Medicare Part C."}
test_id_14.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
