It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_15.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 01, 2025, provides explicit guidance that pharmacy providers cannot seek Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance from dual-eligible members, and that Medicare and Medicaid payments must be accepted as payment in full. The newest policy documents, effective July 01, 2025, generally state that dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules and Medicaid is the payer of last resort, but do not detail co-payment collection specifics. Therefore, the provider manual offers more direct and recent guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members and should receive more weight by default.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source, the New York State Medicaid program updated its Telehealth Policy Manual on July 1, 2025, emphasizing that "duals are subject to Medicare rules" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:3 — July 01, 2025]. An earlier policy document from February 2023 states that "Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted" and that "Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" for dual-eligible enrollees [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 01, 2023]. For immunization services, dual-eligible members are directed to "continue to access immunization services through Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 01, 2021]. Policy documents do not explicitly define co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members in terms of who pays the co-payment or if Medicaid covers it, beyond stating that Medicare rules apply and Medicaid is secondary.
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, the New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, effective October 01, 2025, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit "cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Me [...]" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. Furthermore, it states that "The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. For Medicare Part D members, Medicaid generally only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under Part D plans, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources indicate that Medicare is the primary payer for services for dual-eligible members, with policy stating "duals are subject to Medicare rules" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:3 — July 01, 2025] and "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 01, 2023]. The provider manual implies this by detailing how Medicare and Medicaid payments are handled for pharmacy services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that pharmacy providers of a Medicare Part B benefit "cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Me [...]" for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025], a specific instruction regarding co-payment responsibility not found in the provided policy documents.

The provider manual specifies that "The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025], providing a direct instruction on the finality of payment for dual-eligible members that is not present in the policy excerpts.

The provider manual details Medicaid's limited role in covering specific drug classes excluded from Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025], whereas policy documents generally state duals are subject to Medicare rules without this level of detail for pharmacy benefits.
comparison: Both sources indicate that Medicare is the primary payer for services for dual-eligible members, with policy stating "duals are subject to Medicare rules" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:3 — July 01, 2025] and "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — February 01, 2023]. The provider manual implies this by detailing how Medicare and Medicaid payments are handled for pharmacy services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].

The provider manual explicitly states that pharmacy providers of a Medicare Part B benefit "cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Me [...]" for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025], a specific instruction regarding co-payment responsibility not found in the provided policy documents.

The provider manual specifies that "The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025], providing a direct instruction on the finality of payment for dual-eligible members that is not present in the policy excerpts.

The provider manual details Medicaid's limited role in covering specific drug classes excluded from Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025], whereas policy documents generally state duals are subject to Medicare rules without this level of detail for pharmacy benefits.
answers.caveats: The policy documents provide general principles regarding Medicaid as the payer of last resort and dual-eligible members being subject to Medicare rules. However, they lack specific guidance on the collection of co-payments, deductibles, or coinsurance from dual-eligible members. The provider manual, specifically for pharmacy services, offers explicit instructions prohibiting providers from seeking Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance from these members.

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.

September 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 2

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2025 Volume 41 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

test_id_16.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more recent and specific guidance on Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, stating that Medicaid only covers drugs specifically excluded by law from Part D plans. Policy documents, with the newest relevant guidance from July 1, 2020, offer a general rule that Medicaid will not cover claims if the primary insurer does not, but lack specific details on Part D-excluded drugs. Therefore, the provider manual should receive more weight for current guidance.
answers.policy_definition: Based on the provided policy documents, there is no explicit definition or specific guidance for "Medicare Part D-excluded drugs." However, a general rule states that if the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual guidance, effective October 1, 2025, it is generally not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member, as most drug costs are paid by Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. Medicaid *only* pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources imply a hierarchy of payment responsibility, where another insurer (Medicare Part D in this case) is considered first before Medicaid coverage is considered [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly defines and provides specific examples of drugs that Medicaid *will* cover for Medicare Part D members (i.e., those specifically excluded by law from Part D plans, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents do not provide this specific detail for Part D-excluded drugs.

Policy documents provide a general rule that "If the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01], which could be interpreted broadly, whereas the provider manual offers a specific exception for Part D-excluded drugs.

The provider manual guidance is more recent (effective October 1, 2025) and directly addresses Medicare Part D members and excluded drugs, while the most relevant policy guidance is older (effective July 1, 2020) and provides a general statement about primary insurers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01].
comparison: Both sources imply a hierarchy of payment responsibility, where another insurer (Medicare Part D in this case) is considered first before Medicaid coverage is considered [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly defines and provides specific examples of drugs that Medicaid *will* cover for Medicare Part D members (i.e., those specifically excluded by law from Part D plans, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents do not provide this specific detail for Part D-excluded drugs.

Policy documents provide a general rule that "If the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01], which could be interpreted broadly, whereas the provider manual offers a specific exception for Part D-excluded drugs.

The provider manual guidance is more recent (effective October 1, 2025) and directly addresses Medicare Part D members and excluded drugs, while the most relevant policy guidance is older (effective July 1, 2020) and provides a general statement about primary insurers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01].
answers.caveats: None

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Clarification Regarding Pharmacy and Practitioner Dispensing of
Drugs Requiring Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs require administration by a practitioner and
may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy. Practitioner administered drugs (PADs)
billed directly to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy, are listed on the New York
State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx. The intent of
this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs. Nothing in this policy is
meant to suggest that any or all PADs must be dispensed by a pharmacy.
PADs Billed by a Practitioner
PADs billed by a practitioner using medical claims format should refer to the policy and guidance regarding
PAD billing located on the eMedNY “Physician Manual” web page, at: https://www.emedny.org/Provider
Manuals/Physician/index.aspx.

no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The
Medicaid Program does not provide reimbursement for replacement supplies of lost, stolen, or misdirected
medication, medical/surgical supply or DME deliveries or when confirmation of delivery was made with
delivery industry tracking receipts (where there is no handwritten or electronic signature of receipt by
recipient or authorized agent or designee).
• Once delivered and signed for by a facility or agent of a facility, the site of receipt/handling/administration
is responsible for replacement of improperly stored, handled, lost, or stolen drugs.
• The pharmacy must ensure proper storage is available and authorized agent or recipient is aware of
requirements before delivery.

• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.
• The Department reserves the right to require member or caregiver signatures in situations where there is
high probability of lost or stolen items, or an increase in complaints from members concerning lost or
stolen items.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_17.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, updated more recently on October 1, 2025, explicitly details Medicaid's coverage for Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, whereas the provided policy documents, with the newest relevant update on February 1, 2025, do not offer specific guidance on this particular carve-out. The provider manual should receive more weight by default for current guidance.
answers.policy_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents. The newest policy document, 'New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2' [mu_no02_feb25_pr.pdf::0044 — 2025-02-01], discusses copayment overrides but not the coverage of Part D-excluded drugs. An older policy document, 'New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12' [mu_no12_jul20.pdf::0025 — 2020-07-01], states a general rule that if a primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim, and mentions billing Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs.
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source, 'New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines' [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0070 — 2025-10-01], Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under Medicare Part D plans. Examples of such drugs include select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0070 — 2025-10-01]. Generally, it is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless it falls under these exclusions [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0070 — 2025-10-01]. For dual-eligible members, the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount received for a Medicaid-only patient, and any Medicare and Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0069 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources imply that Medicaid generally acts as a secondary payer or payer of last resort when other insurance, such as Medicare Part D, is available. The policy states, 'If the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim' [mu_no12_jul20.pdf::0025 — 2020-07-01], and the provider manual states, 'It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below' [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0070 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly defines Medicaid's role in covering drugs specifically excluded by law from Medicare Part D plans, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0070 — 2025-10-01]. The provided policy documents do not offer this specific carve-out or definition for Part D-excluded drugs.

The provider manual provides specific examples of drug types that Medicaid will cover when excluded from Part D (e.g., select prescription vitamins, certain non-prescription drugs) [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0070 — 2025-10-01], which is not present in the policy documents.
comparison: Both sources imply that Medicaid generally acts as a secondary payer or payer of last resort when other insurance, such as Medicare Part D, is available. The policy states, 'If the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim' [mu_no12_jul20.pdf::0025 — 2020-07-01], and the provider manual states, 'It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below' [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0070 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly defines Medicaid's role in covering drugs specifically excluded by law from Medicare Part D plans, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0070 — 2025-10-01]. The provided policy documents do not offer this specific carve-out or definition for Part D-excluded drugs.

The provider manual provides specific examples of drug types that Medicaid will cover when excluded from Part D (e.g., select prescription vitamins, certain non-prescription drugs) [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0070 — 2025-10-01], which is not present in the policy documents.
answers.caveats: The provided policy documents lack specific guidance on the handling of Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, making a direct comparison of detailed definitions challenging. The provider manual offers clear and specific instructions on this topic.

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

policy.text:
Pharmacies may use the following override to remove the copayment for these scenarios:

Standard Companion Guide Transaction Information, located at: https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/
5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf;
• eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and Adjudication
ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy
/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20(D.0)
.pdf.

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, explicitly details Medicaid's coverage for Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, while the policy documents, with the newest relevant update effective February 1, 2025, do not offer specific instructions on this topic. The provider manual is more recent and directly addresses the concept, and therefore should receive more weight for current guidance.
answers.policy_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents. The policy documents contain a general rule that "If the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020], but this does not specifically address drugs *excluded by law* from Part D.
answers.provider_manual_definition: Medicaid will only pay for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs if the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under Medicare Part D plans [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]. Examples of such drugs include select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]. Generally, it is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless it falls under this exclusion [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources imply that Medicaid is generally not the primary payer when Medicare Part D is involved, with the policy document stating a general rule that "If the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020], and the provider manual stating it is "not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual explicitly defines and provides specific examples of Medicare Part D-excluded drugs that Medicaid *will* cover, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs, when the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D plans [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy documents do not provide specific guidance or exceptions for Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, only a general rule about primary insurer coverage [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020].
comparison: Both sources imply that Medicaid is generally not the primary payer when Medicare Part D is involved, with the policy document stating a general rule that "If the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020], and the provider manual stating it is "not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual explicitly defines and provides specific examples of Medicare Part D-excluded drugs that Medicaid *will* cover, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs, when the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D plans [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy documents do not provide specific guidance or exceptions for Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, only a general rule about primary insurer coverage [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020].
answers.caveats: The policy documents lack specific guidance on the handling of Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, making a direct comparison of specific rules difficult beyond general principles. The provider manual offers clear and specific instructions for this scenario.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Drugs will be evaluated on a case-by-case basis to determine if they are appropriate to add to the
pharmacy formulary.
Certain practitioner-administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs and may be
billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that practitioner-administered drugs
must be dispensed as a Pharmacy benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician’s Manual found here: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a pharmacy benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to the
site of administration. This is considered “white bagging” and is acceptable under the following guidelines:
• Drugs should only be dispensed by the pharmacy directly to the patient when they are to be self-
administered. The policy surrounding self-administered drug delivery is found in section titled Delivery
above.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_19.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: The New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual, effective October 1, 2025, explicitly defines Medicaid's coverage for Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, whereas the provided policy documents, with the newest relevant update from February 1, 2025, lack specific guidance on this topic. The provider manual is more recent and should receive more weight for current guidance.
answers.policy_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents. The policy documents discuss general third-party liability, stating that if a primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020], but do not specifically address Medicare Part D-excluded drugs.
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]. It is generally not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any other prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, explicitly defines Medicaid's coverage for Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, specifying that Medicaid covers drugs from its List of Reimbursable Drugs that are legally excluded from Part D, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025].
The provided policy documents, with the newest relevant update from February 1, 2025, do not offer specific guidance or definitions regarding Medicaid's handling of Medicare Part D-excluded drugs.
comparison: The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, explicitly defines Medicaid's coverage for Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, specifying that Medicaid covers drugs from its List of Reimbursable Drugs that are legally excluded from Part D, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025].
The provided policy documents, with the newest relevant update from February 1, 2025, do not offer specific guidance or definitions regarding Medicaid's handling of Medicare Part D-excluded drugs.
answers.caveats: While the policy documents include a general statement that Medicaid will not cover a claim if the primary insurer does not cover a medication [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020], the more recent and specific guidance in the provider manual clarifies an exception for drugs legally excluded from Medicare Part D coverage. Therefore, the specific guidance from the provider manual should be considered the controlling authority for Medicare Part D-excluded drugs.

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.

test_id_20.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, offers specific guidance on Medicaid's coverage of drugs legally excluded from Medicare Part D, while the most relevant policy documents from July 1, 2020, provide general rules on Medicaid's secondary payer role. The provider manual is more recent and directly addresses the target concept, and should therefore receive more weight for current guidance.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy document, effective February 1, 2025, does not provide specific guidance on Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, focusing instead on copayment overrides [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025]. Older policy guidance states that if the primary insurer (Medicare) does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020]. Providers are instructed to bill Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs and Part D for Part D covered drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020]. This implies Medicaid is generally secondary and does not cover drugs Medicare *should* cover, but it does not explicitly address drugs *excluded by law* from Part D.
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, it is generally not appropriate to bill Medicaid for prescription drugs for a Medicare Part D member, as most drug costs are paid by Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]. However, Medicaid *will* pay for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is "specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]. Examples of such excluded drugs include "Select prescription vitamins" and "Certain non-prescription drugs" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]. For dual-eligible members, the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the amount for a Medicaid-only patient, and if Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources imply that Medicaid generally acts as a secondary payer when Medicare coverage is available for drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual explicitly defines and provides examples of "Medicare Part D-excluded drugs" that Medicaid *will* cover, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs, when the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy documents, particularly the most relevant ones from July 2020, do not explicitly define or provide specific guidance on Medicaid's coverage for drugs *excluded by law* from Medicare Part D, instead stating a general rule that if Medicare doesn't cover a drug, Medicaid won't either [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020]. The newest policy document (Feb 2025) does not address this specific concept at all [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources imply that Medicaid generally acts as a secondary payer when Medicare coverage is available for drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual explicitly defines and provides examples of "Medicare Part D-excluded drugs" that Medicaid *will* cover, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs, when the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy documents, particularly the most relevant ones from July 2020, do not explicitly define or provide specific guidance on Medicaid's coverage for drugs *excluded by law* from Medicare Part D, instead stating a general rule that if Medicare doesn't cover a drug, Medicaid won't either [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020]. The newest policy document (Feb 2025) does not address this specific concept at all [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: None

• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Additional information regarding the Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Program and residential health care
facilities may be accessed at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/.
See Section 4.0 for Long Term Care Pharmacy Short Cycle Billing information.
Items Provided by Child (Foster) Care Agencies
Please see this link for items provided by Childcare Agencies:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/carveout.htm.
OMH Residential Treatment Facility Prescription Drug Carve-Out
Reimbursement of prescription drugs for children and youth between the ages of five and twenty-one who are
residents of the Office of Mental Health (OMH) Residential Treatment Facilities (RTF) is a NYRx benefit and billed
directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy.
Physician administered drugs, OTC drugs, medical supplies, immunization services (vaccines and their administration),
nutritional supplies, sick room supplies, adult diapers, and durable medical equipment (DME) are not carved out of the
RTF rate and remain the responsibility of the facility.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_20.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, offers specific guidance on Medicaid's coverage of drugs legally excluded from Medicare Part D, while the most relevant policy documents from July 1, 2020, provide general rules on Medicaid's secondary payer role. The provider manual is more recent and directly addresses the target concept, and should therefore receive more weight for current guidance.", "policy_definition": "The newest policy document, effective February 1, 2025, does not provide specific guidance on Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, focusing instead on copayment overrides [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025]. Older policy guidance states that if the primary insurer (Medicare) does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020]. Providers are instructed to bill Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs and Part D for Part D covered drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020]. This implies Medicaid is generally secondary and does not cover drugs Medicare *should* cover, but it does not explicitly address drugs *excluded by law* from Part D.", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, it is generally not appropriate to bill Medicaid for prescription drugs for a Medicare Part D member, as most drug costs are paid by Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]. However, Medicaid *will* pay for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is \"specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]. Examples of such excluded drugs include \"Select prescription vitamins\" and \"Certain non-prescription drugs\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]. For dual-eligible members, the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the amount for a Medicaid-only patient, and if Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources imply that Medicaid generally acts as a secondary payer when Medicare coverage is available for drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly defines and provides examples of \"Medicare Part D-excluded drugs\" that Medicaid *will* cover, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs, when the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — Oct 01, 2025].", "The policy documents, particularly the most relevant ones from July 2020, do not explicitly define or provide specific guidance on Medicaid's coverage for drugs *excluded by law* from Medicare Part D, instead stating a general rule that if Medicare doesn't cover a drug, Medicaid won't either [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020]. The newest policy document (Feb 2025) does not address this specific concept at all [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025]."], "caveats": null}
test_id_20.ground_truth:
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."

This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_21.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_21.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an Effective Date of 2025-10-01, is more recent than the newest policy document (Effective Date 2023-02-01) and should guide current practice. Both sources consistently mandate that Medicare must be billed prior to Medicaid, as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy document, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means all other sources of reimbursement, including Medicare, "must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" for dual eligible enrollees, pursuant to federal law [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]. Earlier policy also indicates that for dual eligible members, immunization services are accessed through Medicare, and pharmacies can only bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01]. Pharmacies have a general responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, which includes Medicare or Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual explicitly states that "Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid" and that "Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. This requirement is analogous to Third Party Liability (TPL), where pharmacies must submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01]. The manual further clarifies that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater, no additional payment will be made, reinforcing Medicare as the primary payer [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both the newest policy (Effective Date 2023-02-01) and provider manual (Effective Date 2025-10-01) documents explicitly state that Medicare must be billed prior to Medicaid [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources align with the principle that Medicaid acts as the payer of last resort, requiring other reimbursement sources to be exhausted first [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The policy document specifically frames the requirement for billing Medicare prior to Medicaid in the context of "dual eligible enrollees" and cites "federal law" for Medicaid being the payer of last resort [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01].

The provider manual states the requirement more broadly for "Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid" and emphasizes maximizing Medicare benefits, without explicitly limiting the primary statement to dual eligible enrollees [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual provides more detailed implications of Medicare being billed first, such as payment limits not exceeding the Medicaid-only fee and accepting Medicare and Medicaid payment as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both the newest policy (Effective Date 2023-02-01) and provider manual (Effective Date 2025-10-01) documents explicitly state that Medicare must be billed prior to Medicaid [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources align with the principle that Medicaid acts as the payer of last resort, requiring other reimbursement sources to be exhausted first [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

The policy document specifically frames the requirement for billing Medicare prior to Medicaid in the context of "dual eligible enrollees" and cites "federal law" for Medicaid being the payer of last resort [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01].

The provider manual states the requirement more broadly for "Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid" and emphasizes maximizing Medicare benefits, without explicitly limiting the primary statement to dual eligible enrollees [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual provides more detailed implications of Medicare being billed first, such as payment limits not exceeding the Medicaid-only fee and accepting Medicare and Medicaid payment as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].
answers.caveats: None

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies may enroll in Medicaid as a billing provider (also known as a fee-for-service (FFS) provider) or as a
Managed Care Only (non-billing) provider. Fee-for-service pharmacies require Medicare enrollment. Managed
Care Only enrolled pharmacies are not required to enroll in Medicare. NYS Medicaid does not enroll out of state,
mail order, compounding, infusion, Long Term Care (LTC), or specialty pharmacies as FFS billing providers
unless the NYS Department of Health (DOH) determines an unmet need for such pharmacy enrollment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health
