test_id_21.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_21.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_21.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_22.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_22.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

answers.headline_summary: The New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual (effective October 1, 2025) is more recent than the newest policy document (effective April 1, 2025) and should receive more weight. Both sources consistently state that pharmacies must bill Medicare before billing Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payer of last resort.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source, New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort and federal regulations require all other available resources to be used first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]. This includes Medicare, which must be billed prior to billing Medicaid for dual eligible enrollees, as Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]. It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, including Medicare, before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. Medicare benefits must be maximized prior to billing Medicaid, meaning services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. It is the responsibility of the provider to determine if the patient is covered by other insurance and if that insurance covers the service being billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23].
similarities: Both policy and provider manual documents consistently state that Medicaid is the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources require that Medicare be billed prior to billing Medicaid for services covered by both programs [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

Both emphasize the provider's responsibility in coordinating benefits and determining other insurance coverage [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23].
differences: The provider manual explicitly requires pharmacies enrolled in Medicaid to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01], a specific requirement not found in the provided policy document excerpts.

Policy documents generally refer to billing 'applicable third parties' or 'all other available resources' which 'including Medicare' [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01], whereas the provider manual provides a more direct and specific instruction that 'Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid' and 'Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both policy and provider manual documents consistently state that Medicaid is the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources require that Medicare be billed prior to billing Medicaid for services covered by both programs [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

Both emphasize the provider's responsibility in coordinating benefits and determining other insurance coverage [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23].

The provider manual explicitly requires pharmacies enrolled in Medicaid to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01], a specific requirement not found in the provided policy document excerpts.

Policy documents generally refer to billing 'applicable third parties' or 'all other available resources' which 'including Medicare' [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01], whereas the provider manual provides a more direct and specific instruction that 'Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid' and 'Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.

policy.text:

with Other Payer ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier for Pharmacy
Claims
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.

Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

test_id_22.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_22.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_22.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_23.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_23.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

answers.headline_summary: The New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual (Effective Date: October 1, 2025) is more recent than the newest policy document (Effective Date: April 1, 2025). Both sources consistently state that pharmacies must bill Medicare *before* billing Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, meaning there are no circumstances where Medicaid should be billed prior to Medicare.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy document, New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 1, 2025]. This requirement is reiterated, stating that NYS Medicaid is the payor of last resort and federal regulations mandate all other available resources be used first [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:4 — May 1, 2024]. Pharmacies are responsible for billing to the appropriate party, which includes Medicare, before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 1, 2020].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider must maximize Medicare benefits prior to billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025]. Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025].
similarities: Both sources establish that Medicaid is the 'payer of last resort' [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 1, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:4 — May 1, 2024; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025].

Both sources require that other available resources or applicable third parties, including Medicare, must be billed and maximized *before* billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 1, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 1, 2020; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025].
Neither source indicates any scenario where a pharmacy must bill Medicaid *before* billing Medicare.
differences: While both sources agree that Medicare must be billed before Medicaid, the provider manual explicitly states, 'Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025]. Policy documents use broader language, requiring billing of 'applicable third parties' and 'all other available resources' before Medicaid, with Medicare listed as an 'appropriate party' [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 1, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 1, 2020].
comparison: Both sources establish that Medicaid is the 'payer of last resort' [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 1, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:4 — May 1, 2024; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025].

Both sources require that other available resources or applicable third parties, including Medicare, must be billed and maximized *before* billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 1, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 1, 2020; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025].
Neither source indicates any scenario where a pharmacy must bill Medicaid *before* billing Medicare.

While both sources agree that Medicare must be billed before Medicaid, the provider manual explicitly states, 'Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025]. Policy documents use broader language, requiring billing of 'applicable third parties' and 'all other available resources' before Medicaid, with Medicare listed as an 'appropriate party' [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 1, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 1, 2020].
answers.caveats: None

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
with Other Payer ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier for Pharmacy
Claims
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies may enroll in Medicaid as a billing provider (also known as a fee-for-service (FFS) provider) or as a
Managed Care Only (non-billing) provider. Fee-for-service pharmacies require Medicare enrollment. Managed
Care Only enrolled pharmacies are not required to enroll in Medicare. NYS Medicaid does not enroll out of state,
mail order, compounding, infusion, Long Term Care (LTC), or specialty pharmacies as FFS billing providers
unless the NYS Department of Health (DOH) determines an unmet need for such pharmacy enrollment.

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_24.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_24.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

answers.headline_summary: The New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual (effective 2025-10-01) is more recent than the newest policy document (effective 2025-04-01) and should receive more weight. Both sources consistently state that pharmacies must bill Medicare *before* billing Medicaid, as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source, the New York State Medicaid Update April 2025, NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payer of last resort and federal regulations require all other available resources to be used first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]. For dual-eligible enrollees, potential third-party reimbursement sources, including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]. It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, including Medicare or Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source, the New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines (effective 2025-10-01), pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program must demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program and are required to maximize Medicare benefits prior to billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance, including Medicare, and whether Medicare covers the service being billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18 — 2025-07-23].
similarities: Both the policy documents and the provider manual consistently state that Medicaid is the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01; Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources require Medicare and other third-party payers to be billed *prior to* or *first* before billing Medicaid for services covered by both [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01; Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources place the responsibility on the provider or pharmacy to determine other insurance coverage and bill the appropriate party [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23].
differences: The provider manual explicitly requires pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program to *demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program* [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01], a requirement not explicitly stated in the provided policy documents.

The provider manual uses the phrase "Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01], which implies a more comprehensive effort than the policy's general statement that Medicare "must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01].
comparison: Both the policy documents and the provider manual consistently state that Medicaid is the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01; Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources require Medicare and other third-party payers to be billed *prior to* or *first* before billing Medicaid for services covered by both [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01; Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources place the responsibility on the provider or pharmacy to determine other insurance coverage and bill the appropriate party [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23].

The provider manual explicitly requires pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program to *demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program* [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01], a requirement not explicitly stated in the provided policy documents.

The provider manual uses the phrase "Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01], which implies a more comprehensive effort than the policy's general statement that Medicare "must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01].
answers.caveats: The user's question asks "when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare." However, both the policy documents and provider manual consistently state the opposite: Medicare must be billed *before* Medicaid, as Medicaid is the payer of last resort. The information provided reflects the actual billing order as described in the documents.

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
2

Policy and Billing
Fee-For-Service Reminders from the Bureau of Dental Review
The Need to Bill Third-Party Insurers
Third-party insurers, including Medicare, provide reimbursement for various dental procedures. Since
Medicaid is the payer of last resort, the provider must bill the member’s third-party payers prior to requesting
payment from Medicaid. If the third party is a commercial plan, Medicaid will reimburse the difference only if
the total third-party payment(s) is (are) less than the lesser of the provider’s fee charged to the general public
or the fee developed by New York State Department of Health (DOH) for the specific procedure code. If the
third party is a Medicare Advantage plan, Medicaid will reimburse eighty-five percent (85%) of the patient
responsibility. Prior to initiating treatment which has been approved by a third-party insurance plan, the
provider should obtain a prior approval from Medicaid to ensure that the treatment plan falls within the current
guidelines of the Medicaid Program. Failure to do so may result in the denial of Medicaid benefits for these
services.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2020 Volume 36 Number 14
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Amount Charged (Field 22)
Enter the total amount charged for each service rendered. The amount must not exceed the provider's usual and
customary charge.
Medicare Co-Insurance (Field 23)
When applicable, enter the Medicare co-insurance amount for this drug/supply.
NOTES:
Fields 23, 23A, 23B, and 23C are only applicable if the member is also a Medicare beneficiary.
It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether Medicare covers the service being billed. If the
service is covered or if the provider does not know if the service is covered, the provider must first submit a
claim to Medicare, as Medicaid is always the payer of last resort.
If the provider knows that the service rendered is not covered by Medicare, enter zero in field 23C.
Medicare Deductible (Field 23A)
When applicable, enter the Medicare deductible amount for this drug/supply.

test_id_24.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."

test_id_25.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_25.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides the most current and specific guidance, stating that Medicare benefits must be maximized and services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare. This aligns with the newest policy guidance from April 1, 2025, which generally requires billing all applicable third parties before Medicaid, as Medicaid is the payer of last resort. The provider manual is more up-to-date for this issue and should receive more weight by default.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source, the New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 (effective April 1, 2025), NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill "applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" because "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" and "federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]. Older policy guidance from October 2020 explicitly states that providers "must first bill Medicare or the other third-party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" to ensure Medicaid is the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01]. Similarly, a July 2020 update notes it is the pharmacy's responsibility to bill "the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source, the New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines (effective October 1, 2025), pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. It explicitly states that "Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid" and that "Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. Furthermore, the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made. These payments must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources establish that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01] and [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01], requiring other available resources, including Medicare, to be utilized first [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

Both explicitly state that Medicare must be billed prior to Medicaid for services covered by both programs [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more specific and detailed instructions regarding Medicare/Medicaid reimbursement, including the requirement for pharmacies to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program and the rule that the total payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01].

Policy documents, while stating the general principle of billing third parties first and explicitly mentioning Medicare, do not detail the specific reimbursement limitations or the requirement for pharmacies to demonstrate Medicare participation in the provided excerpts [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01].
comparison: Both sources establish that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01] and [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01], requiring other available resources, including Medicare, to be utilized first [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

Both explicitly state that Medicare must be billed prior to Medicaid for services covered by both programs [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more specific and detailed instructions regarding Medicare/Medicaid reimbursement, including the requirement for pharmacies to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program and the rule that the total payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01].

Policy documents, while stating the general principle of billing third parties first and explicitly mentioning Medicare, do not detail the specific reimbursement limitations or the requirement for pharmacies to demonstrate Medicare participation in the provided excerpts [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01].

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.

This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_25.answer: {"headline_summary": "The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides the most current and specific guidance, stating that Medicare benefits must be maximized and services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare. This aligns with the newest policy guidance from April 1, 2025, which generally requires billing all applicable third parties before Medicaid, as Medicaid is the payer of last resort. The provider manual is more up-to-date for this issue and should receive more weight by default.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy source, the New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 (effective April 1, 2025), NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill \"applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid\" because \"NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort\" and \"federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]. Older policy guidance from October 2020 explicitly states that providers \"must first bill Medicare or the other third-party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid\" to ensure Medicaid is the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01]. Similarly, a July 2020 update notes it is the pharmacy's responsibility to bill \"the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid\" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual source, the New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines (effective October 1, 2025), pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. It explicitly states that \"Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid\" and that \"Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. Furthermore, the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made. These payments must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources establish that Medicaid is the \"payer of last resort\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01] and [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01], requiring other available resources, including Medicare, to be utilized first [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].", "Both explicitly state that Medicare must be billed prior to Medicaid for services covered by both programs [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more specific and detailed instructions regarding Medicare/Medicaid reimbursement, including the requirement for pharmacies to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program and the rule that the total payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01].", "Policy documents, while stating the general principle of billing third parties first and explicitly mentioning Medicare, do not detail the specific reimbursement limitations or the requirement for pharmacies to demonstrate Medicare participation in the provided excerpts [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]."], "caveats": null}
test_id_25.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_25.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_25.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_26.question: For crossover claims, what are the requirements for prior approval after Medicaid payment?
test_id_26.concept: crossover claims prior approval after Medicaid payment

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the policy documents (newest effective date November 1, 2021) and should receive more weight. Neither source provides requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims; instead, both consistently state that prior authorization must be resolved with the primary insurer *prior* to submitting a claim to Medicaid.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source dated November 1, 2021, federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment, and providers must pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — November 01, 2021]. Earlier policy guidance from July 1, 2020, explicitly states that "Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — July 01, 2020]. There is no grounded evidence in the provided policy documents defining requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims.
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment, and providers must pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 01, 2025]. This manual further clarifies that "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit [sic]" to bill the primary payor first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 01, 2025]. When billing for a prescription/order that requires prior approval or prior authorization, the prior approval/authorization number is entered during the billing process [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15, 16 — July 23, 2025]. There is no grounded evidence in the provided provider manual documents defining requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims.
similarities: Both policy and provider manual documents emphasize that providers must first bill Medicare or other third-party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid, ensuring Medicaid is the payor of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — November 01, 2021]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources indicate that prior authorization requirements must be resolved with the third party *prior* to submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — July 01, 2020]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit [sic]" to pursue other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 01, 2025]. This specific phrasing is not present in the provided policy document excerpts.
comparison: Both policy and provider manual documents emphasize that providers must first bill Medicare or other third-party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid, ensuring Medicaid is the payor of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — November 01, 2021]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources indicate that prior authorization requirements must be resolved with the third party *prior* to submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — July 01, 2020]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 01, 2025].

The provider manual explicitly states that "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit [sic]" to pursue other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 01, 2025]. This specific phrasing is not present in the provided policy document excerpts.
answers.caveats: Neither the provided policy documents nor the provider manual documents contain any information or requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims. All references to prior authorization indicate it is a requirement to be fulfilled *prior* to submitting a claim to Medicaid.

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Most items do not require additional authorization prior to dispensing and billing. These items
may be dispensed and billed directly to Medicaid.
• The items in sections 4.4, 4.5, 4.6, and 4.7 of the Durable Medical Equipment, Prosthetics, Orthotics,
Supplies – Procedure Codes and Coverage Guidelines document, found at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/DME_Procedure_Codes.pdf, are not subject
to the carve-out and should continue to be billed to the member’s MC plan.
• There are some DMEPOS items subject to the carve-out, that require pre-approval. To ensure a
smooth transition for Medicaid MC member’s, PA requirements will be temporarily relaxed (e.g.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
November 2021 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 15

Reminder: Pharmacy Billing for Medicaid Patients
with Other Coverage
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to
submitting a claim to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid
member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. As previously
communicated in the October 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled Notification to Providers
of Requirement to Attach Explanation of Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims, available at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no15_oct20.pdf, failure to
obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will deny at point of service with National Council for Prescription
Drug Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization
• Per the September 2015 NYS Medicaid Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Board recommendation and the
November 2018 Medicaid Update article titled, Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service Prior Authorization of
Topical Compounded Drug Products, Prior Authorization (PA) and editing on prescriptions for all topical
compounded drug products is required and will be implemented on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service
(FFS) claims. This process will ensure that compounded topical drug products meet State and Federal

regulations and that the compound ingredients are Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved or
compendia-supported for topical use when submitted to NYRx.
Pharmacies that submit compounds may receive the following edits:
