00551 MR
Payment On Fill Date formulary
PA Required Call Prime
Therapeutics State
Government Solutions. This is
specific to topical compounds.
To obtain a PA, the prescriber
must call the prior
authorization clinical call
Compound Claim Prior Authorization center at 1-877-309-9493.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 12)
If the provider is billing for a prescription/order that requires prior approval or prior authorization, enter the prior
approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order.
Line (Field 12A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 12 applies. If the prior
approval/authorization number entered in field 12 applies to all claim lines, enter an “A”.
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 13)
If a prior approval/authorization number different from the one entered in field 12 applies to another claim line in the
same claim form, enter the other prior approval/authorization number.

Line (Field 13A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 13 applies.
NOTES:
For information regarding how to obtain Prior Approval/Prior Authorization for specific services, please refer
to the Information for All Providers, Inquiry section on the web page for this manual.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_27.question: For crossover claims, what are the requirements for prior approval after Medicaid payment?
test_id_27.concept: crossover claims prior approval after Medicaid payment

answers.headline_summary: The New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual (effective 2025-10-01) is more recent than the newest policy document (effective 2021-11-01). Neither source provides specific requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims; both consistently emphasize that prior authorization with the primary insurer must be resolved *before* submitting a claim to Medicaid.
answers.policy_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims. Policy documents consistently state that any claim issues, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, must be resolved with the third party *prior* to submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. Providers are required to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid, in accordance with federal regulations and 18NYCRR §540.6(e) [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — 2021-11-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims. The provider manual emphasizes that federal regulations and Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require providers to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. It explicitly states that "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit[y to pursue other coverage prior to submitting to Medicaid]" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. Pharmacies have a responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements *before* submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources emphasize that prior authorization requirements with the third-party payor must be resolved *prior* to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources state that providers must pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid, adhering to federal regulations and 18NYCRR §540.6(e), which establishes Medicaid as the payor of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — 2021-11-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit[y to pursue other coverage prior to submitting to Medicaid]" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01], a specific consequence not explicitly detailed in the provided policy documents regarding prior authorization failure.

The provider manual explicitly outlines the responsibility of pharmacies to submit claims to other coverage *before* Medicaid and allows for resubmission to Medicaid *after* proper adjudication with the TPL [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01], whereas the policy documents generally state providers must bill third-party insurance first without specific mention of the resubmission process post-TPL adjudication.
comparison: Both sources emphasize that prior authorization requirements with the third-party payor must be resolved *prior* to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources state that providers must pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid, adhering to federal regulations and 18NYCRR §540.6(e), which establishes Medicaid as the payor of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — 2021-11-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit[y to pursue other coverage prior to submitting to Medicaid]" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01], a specific consequence not explicitly detailed in the provided policy documents regarding prior authorization failure.

The provider manual explicitly outlines the responsibility of pharmacies to submit claims to other coverage *before* Medicaid and allows for resubmission to Medicaid *after* proper adjudication with the TPL [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01], whereas the policy documents generally state providers must bill third-party insurance first without specific mention of the resubmission process post-TPL adjudication.
answers.caveats: The provided documents consistently address prior authorization requirements *before* Medicaid payment as part of the Third-Party Liability (TPL) process, not *after* Medicaid payment. Therefore, there is insufficient evidence to define requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims.

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

November 2021 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 15

Reminder: Pharmacy Billing for Medicaid Patients
with Other Coverage
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to
submitting a claim to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid
member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. As previously
communicated in the October 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled Notification to Providers
of Requirement to Attach Explanation of Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims, available at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no15_oct20.pdf, failure to
obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will deny at point of service with National Council for Prescription
Drug Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”.

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization
• Per the September 2015 NYS Medicaid Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Board recommendation and the
November 2018 Medicaid Update article titled, Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service Prior Authorization of
Topical Compounded Drug Products, Prior Authorization (PA) and editing on prescriptions for all topical
compounded drug products is required and will be implemented on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service
(FFS) claims. This process will ensure that compounded topical drug products meet State and Federal

regulations and that the compound ingredients are Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved or
compendia-supported for topical use when submitted to NYRx.
Pharmacies that submit compounds may receive the following edits:
Edit# Edit Description NCPDP Reject Reject Message Notes/Resolutions
Code
Item Not Eligible For Product not on
00551 MR
Payment On Fill Date formulary
PA Required Call Prime
Therapeutics State
Government Solutions. This is
specific to topical compounds.
To obtain a PA, the prescriber
must call the prior
authorization clinical call
Compound Claim Prior Authorization center at 1-877-309-9493.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.

test_id_27.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_27.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_27.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_28.question: For crossover claims, what are the requirements for prior approval after Medicaid payment?
test_id_28.concept: crossover claims prior approval after Medicaid payment

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, and the newest relevant policy document, effective July 1, 2020, both indicate that prior approval requirements for crossover claims must be resolved with the primary insurer or third-party payor *before* submitting the claim to Medicaid. Neither source outlines requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment, as PA is a prerequisite to Medicaid involvement. The provider manual is significantly more recent and should receive more weight for current guidance.
answers.policy_definition: For crossover claims, any prior authorization (PA) requirements must be resolved with the third party *prior* to submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will result in denial of the claim by Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. If the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. Providers are required to first bill Medicare or other third-party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid to ensure Medicaid is the payor of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: For crossover claims, federal regulations mandate that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment, requiring providers to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility to pursue payment from the responsible third party [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per that plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid, and may only resubmit the claim to Medicaid after it is properly adjudicated with the Third Party Liability (TPL) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources consistently state that prior authorization requirements for crossover claims must be addressed and resolved with the primary insurer or third-party payor *before* submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources emphasize that Medicaid serves as the payor of last resort, necessitating that other available coverage be billed first [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit[y]" to pursue third-party liability [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01], a specific detail not explicitly present in the provided policy excerpts.

The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, offers more recent guidance and provides specific instructions for pharmacies, including advising prescribers and members of potential delays and resubmitting to Medicaid only *after* proper TPL adjudication [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both sources consistently state that prior authorization requirements for crossover claims must be addressed and resolved with the primary insurer or third-party payor *before* submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources emphasize that Medicaid serves as the payor of last resort, necessitating that other available coverage be billed first [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit[y]" to pursue third-party liability [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01], a specific detail not explicitly present in the provided policy excerpts.

The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, offers more recent guidance and provides specific instructions for pharmacies, including advising prescribers and members of potential delays and resubmitting to Medicaid only *after* proper TPL adjudication [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01].
answers.caveats: Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents provide requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims. The information consistently indicates that prior authorization is a prerequisite that must be handled by the primary insurer or third-party payor *before* Medicaid is billed.

with the designated category of service of “0163” (ordered ambulatory diagnostic and treatment
center) or “0282” (hospital-based ordered ambulatory) may bill the invoiced LARC cost as an ordered
ambulatory service.

Neither source outlines requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment, as PA is a prerequisite to Medicaid involvement. The provider manual is significantly more recent and should receive more weight for current guidance.", "policy_definition": "For crossover claims, any prior authorization (PA) requirements must be resolved with the third party *prior* to submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. If the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. Providers are required to first bill Medicare or other third-party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid to ensure Medicaid is the payor of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "For crossover claims, federal regulations mandate that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment, requiring providers to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility to pursue payment from the responsible third party [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per that plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid, and may only resubmit the claim to Medicaid after it is properly adjudicated with the Third Party Liability (TPL) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources consistently state that prior authorization requirements for crossover claims must be addressed and resolved with the primary insurer or third-party payor *before* submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources emphasize that Medicaid serves as the payor of last resort, necessitating that other available coverage be billed first [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly states that \"Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit[y]\" to pursue third-party liability [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01], a specific detail not explicitly present in the provided policy excerpts.", "The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, offers more recent guidance and provides specific instructions for pharmacies, including advising prescribers and members of potential delays and resubmitting to Medicaid only *after* proper TPL adjudication [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01]."], "caveats": "Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents provide requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims. test_id_28.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_28.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_28.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_29.question: For crossover claims, what are the requirements for prior approval after Medicaid payment?
test_id_29.concept: crossover claims prior approval after Medicaid payment

answers.headline_summary: The New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual (effective October 01, 2025) is more recent than the newest policy document (effective November 01, 2021). Neither source provides specific requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims; instead, both consistently emphasize that prior authorization and billing of other coverage must be completed *before* submitting a claim to Medicaid.
answers.policy_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims. Policy documents consistently state that providers must resolve any prior authorization (PA) requirements with the third party *prior* to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — July 01, 2020]. Federal regulations and Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require providers to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid, ensuring Medicaid is the payor of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — November 01, 2021].
answers.provider_manual_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims. The provider manual, effective October 01, 2025, states that federal regulations and Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require providers to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 01, 2025]. It explicitly notes that "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit[y]" to pursue other coverage first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 01, 2025]. Pharmacies are responsible for submitting claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per that plan’s requirements *before* submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — October 01, 2025].
similarities: Both policy and provider manual documents emphasize that prior authorization (PA) requirements must be resolved with the third-party payor *prior* to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — July 01, 2020; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources cite federal regulations and Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) as requiring providers to pursue any available other coverage *prior* to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — November 01, 2021; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 01, 2025].
Neither source provides information or requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims.
differences: The provider manual documents are significantly more recent, with an effective date of October 01, 2025, compared to the newest policy document's effective date of November 01, 2021.

The provider manual explicitly states that "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit[y]" to pursue other coverage first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 01, 2025], a specific phrasing not found in the provided policy excerpts.
comparison: Both policy and provider manual documents emphasize that prior authorization (PA) requirements must be resolved with the third-party payor *prior* to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — July 01, 2020; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources cite federal regulations and Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) as requiring providers to pursue any available other coverage *prior* to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — November 01, 2021; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 01, 2025].
Neither source provides information or requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims.
The provider manual documents are significantly more recent, with an effective date of October 01, 2025, compared to the newest policy document's effective date of November 01, 2021.

The provider manual explicitly states that "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit[y]" to pursue other coverage first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — October 01, 2025], a specific phrasing not found in the provided policy excerpts.
answers.caveats: Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents provide specific requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims. All provided information consistently directs that prior authorization and billing of other coverage must occur *before* Medicaid considers payment. Therefore, the user's specific question about 'after Medicaid payment' cannot be fully answered with the provided context.

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
November 2021 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 15

Reminder: Pharmacy Billing for Medicaid Patients
with Other Coverage
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to
submitting a claim to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid
member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. As previously
communicated in the October 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled Notification to Providers
of Requirement to Attach Explanation of Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims, available at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no15_oct20.pdf, failure to
obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will deny at point of service with National Council for Prescription
Drug Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”.

https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/DME_Procedure_Codes.pdf, are not subject

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization
• Per the September 2015 NYS Medicaid Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Board recommendation and the
November 2018 Medicaid Update article titled, Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service Prior Authorization of
Topical Compounded Drug Products, Prior Authorization (PA) and editing on prescriptions for all topical
compounded drug products is required and will be implemented on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service
(FFS) claims. This process will ensure that compounded topical drug products meet State and Federal

regulations and that the compound ingredients are Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved or
compendia-supported for topical use when submitted to NYRx.
Pharmacies that submit compounds may receive the following edits:
Edit# Edit Description NCPDP Reject Reject Message Notes/Resolutions
Code
Item Not Eligible For Product not on
00551 MR
Payment On Fill Date formulary
PA Required Call Prime
Therapeutics State
Government Solutions. This is
specific to topical compounds.
To obtain a PA, the prescriber
must call the prior
authorization clinical call
Compound Claim Prior Authorization center at 1-877-309-9493.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.

The provider manual documents are significantly more recent, with an effective date of 2025-10-01 for policy guidelines, compared to the newest relevant policy document effective 2023-04-01 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:6, 7 — 2023-04-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility" to pursue other coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01], a specific phrasing not found in the provided policy excerpts.
answers.caveats: The provided documents do not contain information regarding requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims. All references to prior authorization and third-party billing requirements pertain to actions *before* a claim is submitted to Medicaid.

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Most items do not require additional authorization prior to dispensing and billing. These items
may be dispensed and billed directly to Medicaid.
• The items in sections 4.4, 4.5, 4.6, and 4.7 of the Durable Medical Equipment, Prosthetics, Orthotics,
Supplies – Procedure Codes and Coverage Guidelines document, found at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/DME_Procedure_Codes.pdf, are not subject
to the carve-out and should continue to be billed to the member’s MC plan.
• There are some DMEPOS items subject to the carve-out, that require pre-approval. To ensure a
smooth transition for Medicaid MC member’s, PA requirements will be temporarily relaxed (e.g.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Prior approval (PA) is not required for any LARC, or other birth control method, device, or supply covered by
NYS Medicaid FFS. NYS Medicaid FFS reimbursement for LARC is based on the acquisition cost of the
provider (actual, invoiced cost). For important claims processing rules, providers should refer to the
Reasonability Edits for Practitioner-Administered Drugs article published in the August 2015 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2015/
august15_mu.pdf. Additional information can be found in the eMedNY New York State Medicaid Program
Physician – Procedure Codes – Section 2 – Medicine, Drugs, and Drug Administration manual, located at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/PDFS/Physician_Procedure_Codes_Sect2.pdf.
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

Additional Information:
• Hospital outpatient department clinics and free-standing diagnostic and treatment centers (D&TCs)
with the designated category of service of “0163” (ordered ambulatory diagnostic and treatment
center) or “0282” (hospital-based ordered ambulatory) may bill the invoiced LARC cost as an ordered
ambulatory service.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 12)
If the provider is billing for a prescription/order that requires prior approval or prior authorization, enter the prior
approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order.
Line (Field 12A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 12 applies. If the prior
approval/authorization number entered in field 12 applies to all claim lines, enter an “A”.
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 13)
If a prior approval/authorization number different from the one entered in field 12 applies to another claim line in the
same claim form, enter the other prior approval/authorization number.

Line (Field 13A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 13 applies.
NOTES:
For information regarding how to obtain Prior Approval/Prior Authorization for specific services, please refer
to the Information for All Providers, Inquiry section on the web page for this manual.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_30.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_30.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_30.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_31.question: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document, effective April 1, 2025, and provides more specific requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB) in the context of pharmacy claims and Workers' Compensation, which should receive more weight for current guidance. Both sources consistently establish NYS Medicaid as the payer of last resort.
answers.policy_definition: NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations mandate that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01]. Through COB, NYS Medicaid ensures the correct party pays first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01]. When Medicaid does pay, it pays the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — 2022-10-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: For pharmacy claims, if a member has a work-related injury with an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’ Compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. If a drug claim is not related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case (e.g., a maintenance medication for an unrelated chronic condition), NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field 309-C9 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources establish that NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

Both implicitly require that other available resources (third parties or Workers' Compensation) be utilized before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual provides specific instructions for COB related to Workers’ Compensation cases for pharmacy claims, including when Workers' Compensation must be utilized and when NYRx may be billed as primary with a specific Eligibility Clarification Code [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

The policy documents state that NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — 2022-10-01], a detail not present in the provided provider manual excerpts.

The policy documents provide a general requirement for all NYS Medicaid providers to bill applicable third parties [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01], while the provider manual focuses specifically on pharmacy claims and Workers' Compensation [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both sources establish that NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

Both implicitly require that other available resources (third parties or Workers' Compensation) be utilized before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual provides specific instructions for COB related to Workers’ Compensation cases for pharmacy claims, including when Workers' Compensation must be utilized and when NYRx may be billed as primary with a specific Eligibility Clarification Code [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

The policy documents state that NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — 2022-10-01], a detail not present in the provided provider manual excerpts.

The policy documents provide a general requirement for all NYS Medicaid providers to bill applicable third parties [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01], while the provider manual focuses specifically on pharmacy claims and Workers' Compensation [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].
answers.caveats: The provider manual excerpts focus specifically on Coordination of Benefits within the context of the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program and Workers' Compensation, offering granular instructions for these scenarios. The policy documents provide a broader, general requirement for COB across all Medicaid providers. Given the provider manual's later effective date (October 1, 2025) compared to the newest policy document (April 1, 2025), its specific guidance for pharmacy claims should be considered the most current and controlling for that context.

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
May 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

Update to New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits Submission
NYRx, the New York State (NYS) Medicaid Pharmacy program, needs to ensure accurate Coordination of
Benefits (COB) submissions are recognized and other patient responsibility amounts are accepted. This is an
update to previous guidance issued in the Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits (COB) Submission article published in the July 2017 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/jul17_
mu.pdf.
Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. If
there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the patients' health benefits, such as a health
insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient
Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text: If all three conditions are not met, the billing provider may call the Department for assistance.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Maintain inventory of commonly dispensed medications and supplies (e.g., diabetic), that are applicable to
the patients they serve.
• Maintain adequate hours of operation, as needed for the Medicaid members that they serve.
• Are able to respond to urgent or emergent issues that occur after normal operating hours.
• Out of state pharmacies:
o must maintain NY non-resident registration.
o must maintain state registration in service location.
o must maintain Pharmacy / Wholesale Distributor / Manufacturer License Verification with the NYS
Education Department.
o must maintain Medicaid enrollment in service location state.
• Update the Department as required for pharmacy enrollment changes as per the General Policy Guidelines.
• Maintain all required accreditations from a CMS-approved organization in compliance with CMS
regulations.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
To qualify for Medicaid payment, a compounded prescription must include:
• A combination of any two (2) or more legend drugs found on the list of Medicaid Reimbursable Prescription
Drugs, or
• A combination of any legend drug(s) included on the list of Medicaid Reimbursable Prescription Drugs and any
other item(s) not commercially available as an ethical or proprietary product(s) or
• A combination of two (2) or more products which are labeled “Caution: For Manufacturing Purposes Only”.
For example:
• The combination of Aquaphor and Hydrocortisone Cream 2.5% is NOT considered a compound since it does
not meet any of the above requirements.
• Intravenous prescription products that require reconstitution, further measurement, dilution and/or instillation
into a suitable device (i.e., mini-bag, IV reservoir or syringe) for administration are not considered to have been
compounded and should not be submitted for reimbursement as compounds.
NYS Medicaid compound policy:
• Only the dispensing pharmacy may prepare the prescribed compounded prescription.
• Dispensing outsourced prepared compounds is not allowable.
• Compounds trademarked by pharmacies are not coverable.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

"It is the pharmacy's duty to make ""reasonable efforts"" to determine if a member has other primary insurance. It suggests asking the member or checking their eligibility information. The guidance is general and focuses on the principle

The manual requires that when Medicaid is billed as a secondary payer, the claim must indicate the amount paid by the primary insurer. It lacks the specific coding instructions needed for an electronic claim submission
