test_id_38.question: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_38.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, offers more recent and specific guidance on pharmacist responsibilities for missing patient or prescriber information compared to the policy documents, with the newest relevant policy effective January 1, 2025. Therefore, the provider manual should receive more weight by default for current guidance.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy document, "New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1" (effective 2025-01-01), for prescriptions written as part of an emergency department visit where the assigned Primary Care Provider (PCP) is unavailable (e.g., after hours) to verify, the pharmacy is responsible for "using professional judgement in filling prescriptions without regard to restriction requirements" and for "contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding the prescriptions obtained by the recipient" [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01]. Other policy documents discuss general pharmacist responsibilities, such as notifying the prescriber for non-preferred drugs requiring prior authorization [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01], but do not specifically address missing patient or prescriber information on a prescription in the same context as the provider manual.
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, "New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines" (effective 2025-10-01), if the "address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing," the pharmacist is "not required to enter any of these items on the prescription" if the information is "otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is requesting that the prescription be filled," or if "The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01]. Additionally, if the "prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist," the pharmacist is "responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources imply a responsibility for pharmacists to exercise professional judgment or make good faith efforts when information is not immediately complete or available. The policy document mentions "professional judgement in filling prescriptions" for emergency situations [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01], and the provider manual mentions being "otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate" and making a "good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual provides specific guidance for missing patient demographic information (address, age, CIN) and unknown prescriber identity, outlining conditions under which entry is not required and when verification efforts are needed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01].

The policy documents, specifically the newest relevant one, focus on the scenario of an unavailable PCP for emergency department prescriptions, requiring professional judgment to fill and subsequent contact with the PCP [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that pharmacists are *not required to enter* certain missing patient details under specific conditions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01], whereas the policy document focuses on actions to take (fill, contact PCP) rather than data entry requirements.
comparison: Both sources imply a responsibility for pharmacists to exercise professional judgment or make good faith efforts when information is not immediately complete or available. The policy document mentions "professional judgement in filling prescriptions" for emergency situations [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01], and the provider manual mentions being "otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate" and making a "good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual provides specific guidance for missing patient demographic information (address, age, CIN) and unknown prescriber identity, outlining conditions under which entry is not required and when verification efforts are needed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01].

The policy documents, specifically the newest relevant one, focus on the scenario of an unavailable PCP for emergency department prescriptions, requiring professional judgment to fill and subsequent contact with the PCP [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that pharmacists are *not required to enter* certain missing patient details under specific conditions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01], whereas the policy document focuses on actions to take (fill, contact PCP) rather than data entry requirements.
answers.caveats: None

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
A supervising pharmacist must adhere to the following
responsibilities:
• The supervising pharmacist is responsible for the proper conduct of the pharmacy.
• The supervising pharmacist oversees the practice of other employed pharmacists and ensures that
those unlicensed persons do not engage in unlawful activity.
• The supervising pharmacist must have an active role in the conduct of the pharmacy and not a passive
role.
• The supervising pharmacist may not assign their responsibility to others.
• The supervising pharmacist must be aware of all laws and regulations and must instruct and direct
others to ensure compliance.
• The supervising pharmacist must work full-time (at least 30 hours per week). If the establishment is
operating less than 30 hours per week, the supervising pharmacist must work the majority of the hours.
• If the supervising pharmacist is to be absent from a pharmacy at any time, the supervising pharmacist
must ensure another qualified pharmacist is present in their absence.
• The supervising pharmacist cannot supervise more than one pharmacy establishment at a time.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement in filling prescriptions without
regard to restriction requirements if the prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit
and the assigned PCP is unavailable (i.e., after hours) to verify the prescriptions. The pharmacy is responsible
for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding the prescriptions obtained by the recipient.
An assigned primary inpatient hospital is responsible for providing all non-emergency inpatient services to the
restricted recipient except for services provided pursuant to an authorized referral.
Emergency Pharmacy Situations
In the event of an emergency, such as drug shortages, unexpected pharmacy closures, and/or unexpected
travel, providers and pharmacies must refer to the instructions and contact information provided below.
During normal business hours, the restricted recipient can obtain a temporary authorization to fill the
prescription at an alternate pharmacy location by contacting:
• the New York State (NYS) Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG)
Call (518) 474-6866 or email omig.sm.rrp@omig.ny.gov (available Monday through Friday
from 8 a.m.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).

answers.headline_summary: The Provider Manual (effective October 1, 2025) is more recent and provides more specific guidance on pharmacist responsibilities for missing patient or prescriber information than the Policy documents (newest effective January 1, 2025), and should therefore receive more weight. Pharmacists are generally responsible for verifying unknown prescribers and may fill prescriptions with missing patient data under certain conditions, while also using professional judgment for emergency prescriptions with unavailable PCPs.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy document, the pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgment in filling prescriptions without regard to restriction requirements if they were written as part of an emergency department visit and the assigned PCP is unavailable (i.e., after hours) to verify the prescriptions. The pharmacy is also responsible for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding these prescriptions [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01]. Older policy documents outline general responsibilities for pharmacy owners and supervising pharmacists to ensure recognized standards of pharmacy practice, proper conduct, and oversight of other employed pharmacists [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:12 — 2023-04-01; New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:9 — 2023-05-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, if the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these items on the prescription if the information is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person requesting the fill, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01]. Additionally, the pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources imply a responsibility for pharmacists to ensure the legitimacy and proper handling of prescriptions, even when information is initially incomplete or unavailable.

Both sources emphasize the pharmacist's professional judgment and responsibility in specific scenarios involving missing or unavailable information [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The Provider Manual provides specific conditions under which pharmacists are *not required* to enter missing patient demographic information (address, age, CIN) if it's otherwise available or the pharmacist is satisfied with legitimacy [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01]. The Policy documents do not detail such specific exemptions for missing patient demographics.

The Provider Manual explicitly states the pharmacist's responsibility for making a 'good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01]. The Policy documents do not contain this specific instruction regarding unknown prescribers.

The Policy documents specifically address the scenario of emergency department prescriptions where the assigned PCP is unavailable, requiring the pharmacy to use professional judgment to fill and then contact the PCP the next business day [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01]. The Provider Manual does not detail this specific emergency department scenario.
comparison: Both sources imply a responsibility for pharmacists to ensure the legitimacy and proper handling of prescriptions, even when information is initially incomplete or unavailable.

Both sources emphasize the pharmacist's professional judgment and responsibility in specific scenarios involving missing or unavailable information [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01].

The Provider Manual provides specific conditions under which pharmacists are *not required* to enter missing patient demographic information (address, age, CIN) if it's otherwise available or the pharmacist is satisfied with legitimacy [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01]. The Policy documents do not detail such specific exemptions for missing patient demographics.

The Provider Manual explicitly states the pharmacist's responsibility for making a 'good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01]. The Policy documents do not contain this specific instruction regarding unknown prescribers.

The Policy documents specifically address the scenario of emergency department prescriptions where the assigned PCP is unavailable, requiring the pharmacy to use professional judgment to fill and then contact the PCP the next business day [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01]. The Provider Manual does not detail this specific emergency department scenario.
answers.caveats: None

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
A supervising pharmacist must adhere to the following
responsibilities:
• The supervising pharmacist is responsible for the proper conduct of the pharmacy.
• The supervising pharmacist oversees the practice of other employed pharmacists and ensures that
those unlicensed persons do not engage in unlawful activity.
• The supervising pharmacist must have an active role in the conduct of the pharmacy and not a passive
role.
• The supervising pharmacist may not assign their responsibility to others.
• The supervising pharmacist must be aware of all laws and regulations and must instruct and direct
others to ensure compliance.
• The supervising pharmacist must work full-time (at least 30 hours per week). If the establishment is
operating less than 30 hours per week, the supervising pharmacist must work the majority of the hours.
• If the supervising pharmacist is to be absent from a pharmacy at any time, the supervising pharmacist
must ensure another qualified pharmacist is present in their absence.
• The supervising pharmacist cannot supervise more than one pharmacy establishment at a time.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement in filling prescriptions without
regard to restriction requirements if the prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit
and the assigned PCP is unavailable (i.e., after hours) to verify the prescriptions. The pharmacy is responsible
for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding the prescriptions obtained by the recipient.
An assigned primary inpatient hospital is responsible for providing all non-emergency inpatient services to the
restricted recipient except for services provided pursuant to an authorized referral.
Emergency Pharmacy Situations
In the event of an emergency, such as drug shortages, unexpected pharmacy closures, and/or unexpected
travel, providers and pharmacies must refer to the instructions and contact information provided below.
During normal business hours, the restricted recipient can obtain a temporary authorization to fill the
prescription at an alternate pharmacy location by contacting:
• the New York State (NYS) Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG)
Call (518) 474-6866 or email omig.sm.rrp@omig.ny.gov (available Monday through Friday
from 8 a.m.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• The pharmacy is responsible to replace lost, stolen, or mis-directed drugs at no additional cost to the
member or the Medicaid Program.
• Pharmacy providers who deliver medication without member or authorized individual consent will be
required to accept the return of the medication, credit Medicaid the claim, and destroy those drugs per
State Law.
• All Medicaid claims for drugs not picked up or delivered must be reversed within 60 days or sooner as
required by the mandatory part of New York State’s compliance programs under 18 NYCRR §521.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2019 Volume 35 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_39.ground_truth:
"A prescription must be complete and valid according to New York State Education Law and regulations. It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.

The Medicaid program's billing system assumes this professional responsibility is being met. If the pharmacist adds the patient's date of birth and it matches the Medicaid ID, the claim proceeds. The updates don't need to clarify this standard practice."
test_id_39.pm_ground_truth: A prescription must be complete and valid according to New York State Education Law and regulations. It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.

test_id_40.question: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_40.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

answers.headline_summary: The New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, effective October 1, 2025, is more recent and provides specific guidance on pharmacists' responsibilities when patient or prescriber information is missing, whereas the policy documents, effective June 1, 2023, offer general responsibilities. Therefore, the provider manual should receive more weight by default for current guidance on this specific issue.
answers.policy_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents. The policy documents primarily outline general responsibilities for supervising pharmacists and pharmacy owners, such as ensuring the proper conduct of the pharmacy, maintaining recognized standards of pharmacy practice, and preparing and maintaining contemporaneous records. They do not specifically address responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing from a prescription. For example, a supervising pharmacist is responsible for "the proper conduct of the pharmacy" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:9 — May 01, 2023] and to "ensure: • recognized standards of pharmacy practice are maintained" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:12 — April 01, 2023]. Additionally, pharmacy owners and supervising pharmacists are responsible for "preparing and maintaining contemporaneous records" [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:5 — June 01, 2023].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, pharmacists have specific responsibilities when patient or prescriber information is missing. If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter these items on the prescription if the information is "otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is requesting that the prescription be filled, or • The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — Oct 01, 2025]. Furthermore, if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist, the pharmacist is responsible for "making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources implicitly or explicitly emphasize the pharmacist's overall responsibility for ensuring proper practice and the legitimacy of prescriptions. The policy documents state that a supervising pharmacist is responsible for "the proper conduct of the pharmacy" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:9 — May 01, 2023] and to "ensure: • recognized standards of pharmacy practice are maintained" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:12 — April 01, 2023]. The provider manual specifies that a pharmacist must be "otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — Oct 01, 2025] and make a "good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual directly addresses the specific scenario of missing patient (address, age, CIN) and prescriber information, providing explicit conditions under which a pharmacist is not required to enter missing patient data or must verify prescriber identity [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — Oct 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy documents, while outlining general responsibilities for supervising pharmacists and pharmacy owners, do not provide specific guidance on handling missing patient or prescriber information on prescriptions [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:9 — May 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:5 — June 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:12 — April 01, 2023].

The provider manual is significantly more recent, with an Effective Date of October 1, 2025, compared to the newest policy document's Effective Date of June 1, 2023, making the provider manual the more current guidance for this specific issue.
comparison: Both sources implicitly or explicitly emphasize the pharmacist's overall responsibility for ensuring proper practice and the legitimacy of prescriptions. The policy documents state that a supervising pharmacist is responsible for "the proper conduct of the pharmacy" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:9 — May 01, 2023] and to "ensure: • recognized standards of pharmacy practice are maintained" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:12 — April 01, 2023]. The provider manual specifies that a pharmacist must be "otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — Oct 01, 2025] and make a "good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual directly addresses the specific scenario of missing patient (address, age, CIN) and prescriber information, providing explicit conditions under which a pharmacist is not required to enter missing patient data or must verify prescriber identity [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — Oct 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy documents, while outlining general responsibilities for supervising pharmacists and pharmacy owners, do not provide specific guidance on handling missing patient or prescriber information on prescriptions [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:9 — May 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:5 — June 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:12 — April 01, 2023].

The provider manual is significantly more recent, with an Effective Date of October 1, 2025, compared to the newest policy document's Effective Date of June 1, 2023, making the provider manual the more current guidance for this specific issue.
answers.caveats: None

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Record Retention, Furnishing of Records, and Permitting Audit and Cooperation
with Governmental Agencies
As New York State (NYS) Medicaid-enrolled providers, in accordance with Title 18 of the New York Codes,
Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) §504.3(a) and §504.3(g), located at: https://regs.health.ny.gov/content/
section-5043-duties-provider, the pharmacy owner and the supervising pharmacist are responsible for:
• preparing and maintaining contemporaneous records demonstrating the right to receive payment
under the medical assistance program [refer to 18 NYCRR §504.3(a)];
• keeping for a period of six years from the date the care, services or supplies were furnished, all records
necessary to disclose the nature and extent of services furnished and all information regarding claims
for payment submitted by, or on behalf of, the provider [refer to 18 NYCRR §504.3(a)];
Please note: Providers continue to be subject to any additional record retention requirements
outlined in their prior or current contracts with Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) or Managed
Long Term Care (MLTC) Plans or Subcontractors thereof, including retention for 10 years or
more as delineated therein.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The supervising
pharmacist must be enrolled in NYS Medicaid. For additional information, providers should refer to the NYRx,
The NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program Pharmacy Manual – Policy Guidelines, located at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf.
As a NYS Medicaid provider, the supervising pharmacist is responsible for fulfilling the duties outlined in the
“Duties of the Provider” section of the New York State Medicaid Program: Information for All Providers –
General Policy document, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/PDFS/
Information_for_All_Providers-General_Policy.pdf. This includes adherence to all federal and NYS laws, rules
and regulations, and policies that govern the NYS Medicaid program, including NYS pharmacy law.
Responsibility for conformance with all laws and regulations applicable to the conduct of a pharmacy is placed
upon the ownership of the pharmacy and upon a licensed pharmacist who is designated by the owner as the
supervising pharmacist (i.e., pharmacist in charge)*.

If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter these items on the prescription if the information is \"otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is requesting that the prescription be filled, or • The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — Oct 01, 2025]. Furthermore, if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist, the pharmacist is responsible for \"making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — Oct 01, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources implicitly or explicitly emphasize the pharmacist's overall responsibility for ensuring proper practice and the legitimacy of prescriptions. The policy documents state that a supervising pharmacist is responsible for \"the proper conduct of the pharmacy\" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:9 — May 01, 2023] and to \"ensure: • recognized standards of pharmacy practice are maintained\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:12 — April 01, 2023]. The provider manual specifies that a pharmacist must be \"otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — Oct 01, 2025] and make a \"good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["The provider manual directly addresses the specific scenario of missing patient (address, age, CIN) and prescriber information, providing explicit conditions under which a pharmacist is not required to enter missing patient data or must verify prescriber identity [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — Oct 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — Oct 01, 2025].", "The policy documents, while outlining general responsibilities for supervising pharmacists and pharmacy owners, do not provide specific guidance on handling missing patient or prescriber information on prescriptions [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:9 — May 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:5 — June 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:12 — April 01, 2023].", "The provider manual is significantly more recent, with an Effective Date of October 1, 2025, compared to the newest policy document's Effective Date of June 1, 2023, making the provider manual the more current guidance for this specific issue."], "caveats": null}
test_id_40.ground_truth:
"A prescription must be complete and valid according to New York State Education Law and regulations. It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.

The Medicaid program's billing system assumes this professional responsibility is being met. If the pharmacist adds the patient's date of birth and it matches the Medicaid ID, the claim proceeds. The updates don't need to clarify this standard practice."

It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.

test_id_41.question: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_41.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status

answers.headline_summary: Yes, weight loss drugs are an excluded benefit. According to the newest provider manual documents effective October 1, 2025, compounds for excluded indications are not covered, and drugs for anorexia are explicitly non-reimbursable. The newest policy documents, effective January 1, 2025, also confirm that drugs for weight loss are excluded, particularly for compounds and as agents for FFS and MMC programs, making the provider manual the more current guidance for this issue.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest relevant policy document from April 2024, compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications, "including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01]. An earlier policy document from April 2022 explicitly states that "agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain" are exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — 2022-04-01]. The December 2020 policy also reinforces that compounds for "drugs to treat weight loss" are excluded [New York State Medicaid Update December 2020 Volume 36 Number 18:9, 10 — 2020-12-01]. The January 2025 policy document generally states that certain drugs/drug uses are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — 2025-01-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual documents, effective October 1, 2025, compounds "may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01]. Furthermore, the manual lists "Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia" as an example of drugs/drug uses that are "not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01]. The provided excerpts do not explicitly list "weight loss" as a general non-reimbursable drug use outside of the context of compounds and excluded indications.
similarities: Both sources indicate that compounds made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications are not reimbursable. The policy documents explicitly include "drugs to treat weight loss" in this category [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01], and the provider manual states that "Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources identify "anorexia" as a condition for which drugs are not reimbursable. Policy documents state "agents when used for the treatment of anorexia" are excluded [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — 2022-04-01], and the provider manual lists "Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia" as not reimbursable [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The policy documents explicitly state that "agents when used for the treatment of... weight loss" are excluded benefits for both FFS and MMC program areas [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — 2022-04-01].

The provided provider manual excerpts, while stating that compounds for "excluded indications" are not covered and listing "Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia" as non-reimbursable, do not explicitly list "weight loss" as a general non-reimbursable drug use in the same direct manner as the policy documents [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both sources indicate that compounds made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications are not reimbursable. The policy documents explicitly include "drugs to treat weight loss" in this category [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01], and the provider manual states that "Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources identify "anorexia" as a condition for which drugs are not reimbursable. Policy documents state "agents when used for the treatment of anorexia" are excluded [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — 2022-04-01], and the provider manual lists "Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia" as not reimbursable [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01].

The policy documents explicitly state that "agents when used for the treatment of... weight loss" are excluded benefits for both FFS and MMC program areas [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — 2022-04-01].

The provided provider manual excerpts, while stating that compounds for "excluded indications" are not covered and listing "Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia" as non-reimbursable, do not explicitly list "weight loss" as a general non-reimbursable drug use in the same direct manner as the policy documents [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01].
answers.caveats: The provider manual, while newer, does not explicitly list "weight loss" as a general non-reimbursable drug use in the provided snippets, unlike the policy documents which explicitly state "agents when used for the treatment of... weight loss" are excluded. However, the provider manual's statement that "Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications" implies that if weight loss is an excluded indication (as stated in policy), then compounds for it are also excluded. The policy documents provide a more direct and broader statement regarding "weight loss" as an excluded benefit for agents.

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2020 Volume 36 Number 18
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
*Requires additional MEVS.
Denial code “722”: NDC DESI code is invalid.
NDC Excluded from State Plan Coverage
NYS Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State Department of Health
List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx (unless provided
by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance with policy and/or state or federal
legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2),
located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm, and SOS §365-a(4)(f), located at: https://www.
nysenate.gov/legislation/laws/SOS/365-A.
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Yes, weight loss drugs are an excluded benefit. The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, is more recent than the policy documents, effective January 1, 2025, and both sources consistently indicate that drugs for weight loss are an excluded benefit under NYS Medicaid, with the policy documents providing more explicit detail. The provider manual should receive more weight by default.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy documents, effective January 1, 2025, indications for weight loss are not covered by NYS Medicaid, pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01]. For example, Wegovy for weight loss is listed as a State Plan exclusion [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01]. Policy effective April 1, 2024, further clarifies that compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to, "drugs to treat weight loss" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01]. An earlier policy, effective April 1, 2022, also states that "agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain" are exclusions for both FFS and MMC programs, additionally pursuant to New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3) [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — 2022-04-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01]. While the provided excerpt does not explicitly list "weight loss" in the examples of non-reimbursable drugs, the general prohibition against "excluded indications" implies that if weight loss is an excluded indication per policy, it would also be excluded under the manual.

Both sources refer to federal legislation (SSA §1927(d)(2) in policy, and "state or federal Legislation" in provider manual) as a basis for drug exclusions [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources mention anorexia as a condition for which drug treatment is not reimbursable (explicitly in the provider manual, and linked with weight loss in policy documents) [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — 2022-04-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The policy documents explicitly list "weight loss" as an excluded indication for drugs, providing a specific example like Wegovy for weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01; New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01; New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — 2022-04-01]. The provider manual, in the provided excerpt, explicitly lists "Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia" as non-reimbursable but does not explicitly mention "weight loss" in that specific list of examples [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both the newest policy documents (effective January 1, 2025) and the newest provider manual (effective October 1, 2025) indicate that drugs for excluded indications are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources mention anorexia as a condition for which drug treatment is not reimbursable (explicitly in the provider manual, and linked with weight loss in policy documents) [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — 2022-04-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual, in the provided excerpt, explicitly lists "Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia" as non-reimbursable but does not explicitly mention "weight loss" in that specific list of examples [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01].
answers.caveats: The provider manual, while more recent, does not explicitly list "weight loss" in its examples of non-reimbursable drugs in the provided excerpt, unlike the policy documents which are very explicit. However, the manual's general prohibition against "excluded indications" and its explicit mention of "Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia" (a condition often related to weight management) strongly suggests alignment with the policy documents that weight loss drugs are an excluded benefit.

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_42.answer: {"headline_summary": "Yes, weight loss drugs are an excluded benefit. The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, is more recent than the policy documents, effective January 1, 2025, and both sources consistently indicate that drugs for weight loss are an excluded benefit under NYS Medicaid, with the policy documents providing more explicit detail. The provider manual should receive more weight by default.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy documents, effective January 1, 2025, indications for weight loss are not covered by NYS Medicaid, pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01]. For example, Wegovy for weight loss is listed as a State Plan exclusion [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01]. Policy effective April 1, 2024, further clarifies that compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to, \"drugs to treat weight loss\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01]. An earlier policy, effective April 1, 2022, also states that \"agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain\" are exclusions for both FFS and MMC programs, additionally pursuant to New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3) [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — 2022-04-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01]. The manual also lists \"Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia\" as an example of drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01]. While the provided excerpt does not explicitly list \"weight loss\" in the examples of non-reimbursable drugs, the general prohibition against \"excluded indications\" implies that if weight loss is an excluded indication per policy, it would also be excluded under the manual.", "similarities": ["Both the newest policy documents (effective January 1, 2025) and the newest provider manual (effective October 1, 2025) indicate that drugs for excluded indications are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources refer to federal legislation (SSA §1927(d)(2) in policy, and \"state or federal Legislation\" in provider manual) as a basis for drug exclusions [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources mention anorexia as a condition for which drug treatment is not reimbursable (explicitly in the provider manual, and linked with weight loss in policy documents) [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — 2022-04-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The policy documents explicitly list \"weight loss\" as an excluded indication for drugs, providing a specific example like Wegovy for weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01; New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01; New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — 2022-04-01]. The provider manual, in the provided excerpt, explicitly lists \"Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia\" as non-reimbursable but does not explicitly mention \"weight loss\" in that specific list of examples [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01]."], "caveats": "The provider manual, while more recent, does not explicitly list \"weight loss\" in its examples of non-reimbursable drugs in the provided excerpt, unlike the policy documents which are very explicit. However, the manual's general prohibition against \"excluded indications\" and its explicit mention of \"Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia\" (a condition often related to weight management) strongly suggests alignment with the policy documents that weight loss drugs are an excluded benefit."}
test_id_42.ground_truth:
"The manual provides no specific examples of modern weight loss drugs (like GLP-1 agonists). It relies entirely on the general categorical exclusion, which could be open to interpretation with newer medications.

The examples given are: Wegovy, Ozempic, Qsymia, and Mounjaro. This is the most significant clarification, as it removes all ambiguity regarding these popular medications."
test_id_42.pm_ground_truth: The manual provides no specific examples of modern weight loss drugs (like GLP-1 agonists). It relies entirely on the general categorical exclusion, which could be open to interpretation with newer medications.
test_id_42.policy_ground_truth: The examples given are: Wegovy, Ozempic, Qsymia, and Mounjaro. This is the most significant clarification, as it removes all ambiguity regarding these popular medications.

test_id_43.question: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_43.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status

answers.headline_summary: Weight loss drugs are an excluded benefit under NYS Medicaid. The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, is more recent than the latest policy update from January 1, 2025, and both sources consistently confirm this exclusion, with the provider manual receiving more weight by default.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy update, effective January 1, 2025, indications for weight loss are not covered by NYS Medicaid, citing Wegovy as an example of a "State Plan exclusion" pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01]. Policy effective April 1, 2024, further clarifies that compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications, including "drugs to treat weight loss" as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01]. An earlier policy, effective April 1, 2022, also states that "agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain" are exclusions for both FFS and MMC programs, additionally pursuant to New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3) [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — 2022-04-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are explicitly listed as examples of drugs/drug uses that are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01]. The manual also states that compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both the policy documents and the provider manual explicitly state that drugs used for weight loss are an excluded benefit under NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources reference the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) as a basis for the exclusion of drugs for weight loss indications [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources indicate that compounds may not be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications, including weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The policy documents specifically name "Wegovy" as an example of a drug with indications for weight loss that is a State Plan exclusion [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01], whereas the provided provider manual excerpts do not list specific drug examples for weight loss exclusions.

An older policy document (April 2022) additionally cites New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3) as a basis for excluding agents used for weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — 2022-04-01], which is not explicitly mentioned in the provided provider manual excerpts.
comparison: Both the policy documents and the provider manual explicitly state that drugs used for weight loss are an excluded benefit under NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources reference the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) as a basis for the exclusion of drugs for weight loss indications [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources indicate that compounds may not be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications, including weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01].

The policy documents specifically name "Wegovy" as an example of a drug with indications for weight loss that is a State Plan exclusion [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01], whereas the provided provider manual excerpts do not list specific drug examples for weight loss exclusions.

An older policy document (April 2022) additionally cites New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3) as a basis for excluding agents used for weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — 2022-04-01], which is not explicitly mentioned in the provided provider manual excerpts.
answers.caveats: None

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).
