test_id_44.question: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_44.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status

answers.headline_summary: Yes, weight loss drugs are an excluded benefit according to both sources. The newest provider manual, effective October 01, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document, effective April 01, 2024, and should receive more weight by default for current guidance.
answers.policy_definition: According to the New York State Medicaid Update April 2024, compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications, including, but not limited to, drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — April 01, 2024]. Earlier guidance from April 2022 also states that agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain are excluded for both the FFS and MMC program areas, additionally pursuant to New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3) [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — April 01, 2022].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, effective October 01, 2025, drugs used for the treatment of anorexia are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — October 01, 2025]. For compounded medications, the manual states that compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — October 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources indicate that certain drug categories are excluded from coverage [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — April 01, 2022], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources refer to the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) regarding excluded indications for compounds [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — April 01, 2024], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources explicitly mention 'anorexia' as an excluded condition for drug treatment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — April 01, 2022], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — October 01, 2025].
differences: The policy documents explicitly list 'weight loss' as an excluded indication for agents and for compounds [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — April 01, 2024], [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — April 01, 2022]. The provider manual, in the provided excerpts, explicitly lists 'anorexia' as an excluded drug use but does not explicitly list 'weight loss' for non-compounds [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — October 01, 2025].
The provider manual is more recent, with an effective date of October 01, 2025, compared to the newest policy document's effective date of April 01, 2024.
comparison: Both sources indicate that certain drug categories are excluded from coverage [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — April 01, 2022], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources refer to the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) regarding excluded indications for compounds [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — April 01, 2024], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources explicitly mention 'anorexia' as an excluded condition for drug treatment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — April 01, 2022], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — October 01, 2025].

The policy documents explicitly list 'weight loss' as an excluded indication for agents and for compounds [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — April 01, 2024], [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — April 01, 2022]. The provider manual, in the provided excerpts, explicitly lists 'anorexia' as an excluded drug use but does not explicitly list 'weight loss' for non-compounds [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — October 01, 2025].
The provider manual is more recent, with an effective date of October 01, 2025, compared to the newest policy document's effective date of April 01, 2024.
answers.caveats: While the policy documents explicitly state that agents for weight loss are an excluded benefit, the provided excerpts from the provider manual explicitly list 'anorexia' as an excluded drug use but do not explicitly mention 'weight loss' for non-compounded drugs. However, for compounds, the provider manual refers to 'excluded indications,' which policy documents define to include weight loss, suggesting an implicit exclusion for weight loss drugs in compounds.

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.

policy.text:
April 2022
Volume 38 | Number 4
Reminder: Some Drug Categories are Excluded from Coverage in
Pharmacy and Medical Benefits
The New York State (NYS) Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) programs
cover an extensive drug benefit providing access to medically necessary drugs. There are, however, certain
drug/drug classes that are not covered by Medicaid for certain diagnoses due to federal or state rules as
outlined in both the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located on the Social Security Administration “Payment
for Covered Outpatient Drugs” website, at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm and in the
eMedNY New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines document,
located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2020 Volume 36 Number 18
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_45.question: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_45.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, is more recent and implicitly indicates weight loss drugs are excluded by referencing "excluded indications" and "drugs used for the treatment of anorexia." Policy documents, with the newest explicit mention effective April 1, 2024, explicitly state that drugs for weight loss are an excluded benefit.
answers.policy_definition: According to the New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 (effective April 1, 2024), compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — April 01, 2024]. This is consistent with earlier guidance from the New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 (effective April 1, 2022), which specifies that agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain are excluded for both FFS and MMC programs, pursuant to New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3). [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — April 01, 2022]. The New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 (effective January 1, 2025) generally states that certain drug uses are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid, but the provided excerpt does not explicitly list weight loss drugs. [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — January 01, 2025].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines (effective October 1, 2025), compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2). [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — October 01, 2025]. The manual also lists "Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia" as an example of drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — October 01, 2025]. The provided excerpts do not explicitly list "weight loss" drugs as an excluded benefit.
similarities: Both sources indicate that certain drug uses are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid due to federal or state rules. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — April 01, 2022], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources refer to the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) as a basis for excluded indications. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — April 01, 2024], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources mention "anorexia" in the context of non-reimbursable drug uses. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — April 01, 2022], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — October 01, 2025].
differences: Policy documents explicitly list "drugs to treat weight loss" as an excluded indication. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — April 01, 2024], [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — April 01, 2022].

The provided provider manual excerpts do not explicitly list "weight loss" as an excluded indication, though they refer to "excluded indications" generally and specifically mention "Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — October 01, 2025], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — October 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources indicate that certain drug uses are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid due to federal or state rules. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — April 01, 2022], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources refer to the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) as a basis for excluded indications. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — April 01, 2024], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources mention "anorexia" in the context of non-reimbursable drug uses. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — April 01, 2022], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — October 01, 2025].

Policy documents explicitly list "drugs to treat weight loss" as an excluded indication. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — April 01, 2024], [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — April 01, 2022].

The provided provider manual excerpts do not explicitly list "weight loss" as an excluded indication, though they refer to "excluded indications" generally and specifically mention "Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — October 01, 2025], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — October 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The provider manual, being the more recent source (effective October 1, 2025), refers to "excluded indications as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2)." Given that policy documents (effective April 1, 2024, and April 1, 2022) explicitly state that "drugs to treat weight loss" are excluded under this same federal act, it is highly probable that the provider manual intends to include weight loss drugs as an excluded benefit, even if not explicitly listed in the provided excerpts. Therefore, providers should consider weight loss drugs as an excluded benefit.

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — October 01, 2025]. "The manual provides no specific examples of modern weight loss drugs (like GLP-1 agonists). It relies entirely on the general categorical exclusion, which could be open to interpretation with newer medications.

test_id_46.question: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: The Provider Manual, effective October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy update from February 1, 2024, and should receive more weight. According to the newest Provider Manual, 340B drugs are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost plus a $10.18 professional dispensing fee, while policy updates emphasize submission at actual acquisition cost, adherence to the 340B ceiling price, and specific claim identifiers.
answers.policy_definition:
According to the newest policy update from February 1, 2024, claims for 340B pharmacy drugs will be denied if the ingredient cost submitted exceeds the 340B ceiling price or if invalid codes are submitted. Denied claims must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost, or a non-340B drug may be dispensed with claim level identifiers removed [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01]. The 340B ceiling price is defined as the maximum amount a manufacturer can charge the covered entity for a 340B drug [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01]. 

Older policy guidance from June 1, 2023, states that all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims, when NYS Medicaid is the primary payor, must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01]. For NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims, drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01]. Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all billed drugs, and reimbursement will not be provided if the NDC is not referenced on a claim [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest Provider Manual, effective October 1, 2025, 340B purchased drugs are reimbursed at the "340B Acquisition Cost" plus a "$10.18" professional dispensing fee, regardless of whether NADAC (National Average Drug Acquisition Cost) is available [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01]. The pharmacy is reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01]. Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts, meaning manufacturers are not required to provide both a discounted 340B price and a Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01]. The 340B Ceiling Price is defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate percentage (Unit Rebate Amount or URA) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:44 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources indicate that reimbursement for 340B drugs is tied to the acquisition cost, referring to "actual acquisition cost" [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01] or "340B Acquisition Cost" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources mention the "340B ceiling price" as a relevant factor, with policy stating claims exceeding it will be denied [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01] and the provider manual defining it [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:44 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources require specific identification for 340B drugs, with policy detailing "UD modifier" for FFS and "claim level identifiers" for NYRx/MMC [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01; New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01], and the provider manual generally stating reimbursement is for medication billed via NDC using reimbursement methodology [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The Provider Manual explicitly states the reimbursement formula for 340B purchased drugs as "340B Acquisition Cost" plus a specific "$10.18" professional dispensing fee [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01], whereas policy documents do not explicitly state a dispensing fee amount in the provided excerpts.

Policy documents provide more granular detail on claim submission requirements for 340B drugs, specifying the use of a "UD modifier" for FFS-only claims [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01] and "claim level identifiers" for all NCPDP-submitted 340B claims to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care, or as secondary claims [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01]. The Provider Manual generally refers to billing via NDC using reimbursement methodology [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01].

The newest policy update from February 2024 focuses on denial reasons related to ingredient cost exceeding the 340B ceiling price and invalid codes [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01], while the newest provider manual (October 2025) directly outlines the reimbursement methodology (acquisition cost + dispensing fee) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01].

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The information in this article is intended to advise
providers, who may be new to submitting claims to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, that
all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor must be
submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost.
Claim level identifiers are required for any 340B-purchased drug billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care
(MMC) Plan, or as a secondary claim, whether it is a medical or pharmacy claim. It is the responsibility of the
covered entity (CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with
the 340B stock of the CE for 340B-eligible NYS Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for
these drugs. If rebates are received by the NYS DOH for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
It is the responsibility of the covered entity
(CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with the 340B stock
of the CE for 340B-eligible Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for these drugs. If rebates
are received by the NYS Department of Health (DOH) for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims. Please note: 340B claims billed to Medicaid FFS as secondary claims also
require identifiers, as well as 340B claims billed to MMC Plans.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Ingredient cost submitted exceeds 340B ceiling price
2. Invalid codes submitted for 340B pharmacy drugs
The 340B ceiling price refers to the maximum amount that a manufacturer can charge the covered entity for
the purchase of a 340B drug. A claim submitted to NYS Medicaid that is higher than the 340B ceiling price
will be denied. Claims that are denied must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost. Alternatively, the
pharmacy may dispense a non-340B drug and remove the claim level identifiers and submit at the usual and
customary charge.
Claims for 340B drugs submitted to NYS Medicaid via the NCPDP D.0 format must also adhere to the
following:
• must be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) members and Medicaid Managed
Care (MMC) enrollees; and
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members and MMC enrollees,
net any manufacturer discounts and/or other price reductions.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pricing methodology is systematically determined as follows:
Drug Type If NADAC is available If NADAC is unavailable, Professional Dispensing Fee*
reimburse at: reimburse at:
Lower of NADAC, FUL, Lower of WAC – 17.5%,
Generics SMAC or U&C FUL, SMAC, or U&C $10.18
Lower of NADAC or
Brands U&C Lower of WAC or U&C $10.18
Lower of NADAC, FUL,
OTCs SMAC or U&C Lower of WAC, FUL, $10.18
SMAC, or U&C
340B**Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost 340B Acquisition Cost $10.18
Drug
Note: Claims will pay at the pharmacy's Usual and Customary (U&C) pricing if lower than drug ingredient cost plus
dispensing fee.
* Professional Dispensing Fee applies if the drug meets the definition of Covered Outpatient Drug and is not paid at
U&C.
**When claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim. See subsection 340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
below for more information.
Federal Upper Limit (FUL) is determined by the Secretary of Health and Human Services, a price ceiling used by Centers
for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) to control prices for certain medications paid to pharmacies.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_46.ground_truth:
"Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.

drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity."

It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.

This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity.

test_id_47.question: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides the most current guidance on 340B drug reimbursement, stating they are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost plus a professional dispensing fee. The newest policy update from June 1, 2023, also emphasizes submission at actual acquisition cost, and the provider manual should receive more weight by default due to its later effective date.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy update, 340B-purchased drug claims, when NYS Medicaid is the primary payor, must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01]. For NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims, drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01]. Covered entities are responsible for correctly identifying claims to ensure rebates are not collected for these drugs, and if rebates are received due to incorrect identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the 340B discounts [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:14 — 2021-11-01]. Claims must be properly identified as 340B and submitted at costs in the appropriate fields [New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6:4, 5 — 2019-05-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, 340B Purchased Drugs are reimbursed at the "340B Acquisition Cost" plus a professional dispensing fee of "$10.18" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01]. Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts, meaning manufacturers are not required to provide both a discounted 340B price and a Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01]. Covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for their Medicaid patients and adhere to federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) rules and requirements, including listing the entity [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01]. All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:30, 31 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources indicate that 340B drugs are reimbursed based on their acquisition cost, with policy referring to "actual acquisition cost" [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01] and the provider manual specifying "340B Acquisition Cost" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources emphasize the importance of proper identification of 340B claims to prevent duplicate discounts or rebates. Policy mentions "claim level identifiers" to ensure rebates are not collected [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01; New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:14 — 2021-11-01], and the provider manual states that "Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states a professional dispensing fee of "$10.18" for 340B purchased drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01], which is not detailed in the provided policy excerpts regarding 340B reimbursement.

Policy documents provide specific claim submission instructions, such as appending the "UD modifier" for NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01] and requiring claim level identifiers for NCPDP-submitted 340B claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor, or for Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) Plan or secondary claims [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01]. The provider manual mentions the need for covered entities to determine 340B usage for Medicaid members and follow HRSA rules but does not detail specific modifiers or submission requirements for different claim types in the provided chunks [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both sources indicate that 340B drugs are reimbursed based on their acquisition cost, with policy referring to "actual acquisition cost" [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01] and the provider manual specifying "340B Acquisition Cost" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01].

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
It is the responsibility of the covered entity
(CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with the 340B stock
of the CE for 340B-eligible Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for these drugs. If rebates
are received by the NYS Department of Health (DOH) for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims. Please note: 340B claims billed to Medicaid FFS as secondary claims also
require identifiers, as well as 340B claims billed to MMC Plans.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The information in this article is intended to advise
providers, who may be new to submitting claims to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, that
all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor must be
submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost.
Claim level identifiers are required for any 340B-purchased drug billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care
(MMC) Plan, or as a secondary claim, whether it is a medical or pharmacy claim. It is the responsibility of the
covered entity (CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with
the 340B stock of the CE for 340B-eligible NYS Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for
these drugs. If rebates are received by the NYS DOH for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
4

Pharmacy
Reminder: 340B Providers, Covered Entities, and Contract Pharmacies
A review of 340B claims submitted via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format
indicates confusion as to which field should contain the ingredient cost and which should contain the Usual and
Customary (U&C). 340B drug claims need to be properly identified as 340B as well as submitted at costs in the
appropriate fields. Claims submitted with incorrect data, including cost in the wrong field, are subject to
audit and recovery.
The following fields are required on each Medicaid 340B drug claim submitted NCPDP format:
Medicaid Primary Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field
Claim (Primary: Medicare; Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 Not applicable
409-D9, Ingredient Cost Submitted * 340B Acquisition
Not applicable
Cost
426-DQ, Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) * Lowest Net Charge Lowest Net Charge to Cash
to Cash Customers Customers
*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pricing methodology is systematically determined as follows:
Drug Type If NADAC is available If NADAC is unavailable, Professional Dispensing Fee*
reimburse at: reimburse at:
Lower of NADAC, FUL, Lower of WAC – 17.5%,
Generics SMAC or U&C FUL, SMAC, or U&C $10.18
Lower of NADAC or
Brands U&C Lower of WAC or U&C $10.18
Lower of NADAC, FUL,
OTCs SMAC or U&C Lower of WAC, FUL, $10.18
SMAC, or U&C
340B**Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost 340B Acquisition Cost $10.18
Drug
Note: Claims will pay at the pharmacy's Usual and Customary (U&C) pricing if lower than drug ingredient cost plus
dispensing fee.
* Professional Dispensing Fee applies if the drug meets the definition of Covered Outpatient Drug and is not paid at
U&C.
**When claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim. See subsection 340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
below for more information.
Federal Upper Limit (FUL) is determined by the Secretary of Health and Human Services, a price ceiling used by Centers
for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) to control prices for certain medications paid to pharmacies.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_47.ground_truth:
"Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.

drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity."
test_id_47.pm_ground_truth: Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.
test_id_47.policy_ground_truth: drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity.

test_id_48.question: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: The New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides the most recent and specific guidance on 340B drug reimbursement, stating they are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost plus a professional dispensing fee. Policy updates, with the newest effective June 1, 2023, emphasize submission at actual acquisition cost with required claim level identifiers. The provider manual should receive more weight due to its later effective date.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy update effective June 1, 2023, all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims, when NYS Medicaid is the primary payor, must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01]. Claim level identifiers are required for any 340B-purchased drug billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) Plan, or as a secondary claim, whether it is a medical or pharmacy claim [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01]. An older policy update from July 1, 2022, specifies that drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims, and if the National Drug Code (NDC) is not referenced, NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01]. Furthermore, covered entities are responsible for correctly identifying 340B claims to ensure rebates are not collected for these drugs, and if rebates are received due to incorrect or missing identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the 340B discounts [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:14 — 2021-11-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, 340B-purchased drugs are reimbursed at the '340B Acquisition Cost' plus a professional dispensing fee of $10.18 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01]. For Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in the NCPDP format, the 'Ingredient Cost Submitted' (field 409-D9) must be the '340B Acquisition Cost' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01]. Additionally, specific claim level identifiers are required, including Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 (field 420-DK) for both primary and secondary claims, and Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 (field 423-DN) for primary claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01]. Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts, meaning manufacturers are not required to provide both a discounted 340B price and a Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01]. Applying claim level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims is considered fraudulent billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources emphasize the importance of identifying 340B drugs on claims to ensure proper processing and prevent issues like duplicate discounts/rebates [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources indicate that reimbursement is based on the 'acquisition cost' or 'actual acquisition cost' of the 340B drug [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources require the use of specific claim level identifiers when submitting 340B drug claims [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly details the reimbursement amount as the '340B Acquisition Cost' plus a specific professional dispensing fee of $10.18 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01], whereas policy documents primarily state the requirement to submit at 'actual acquisition cost' without detailing the full reimbursement calculation [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01].

Policy documents mention the 'UD modifier' for FFS-only claims (effective July 1, 2022) [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01], while the provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, specifies particular NCPDP fields for claim level identifiers, such as Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 and Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that applying claim level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims is considered fraudulent billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01], a specific warning not found in the provided policy excerpts.

The provider manual explicitly references the federal law prohibiting duplicate discounts and the role of the federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01], while policy documents focus on the covered entity's responsibility to prevent duplicate rebates [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:14 — 2021-11-01].
comparison: Both sources emphasize the importance of identifying 340B drugs on claims to ensure proper processing and prevent issues like duplicate discounts/rebates [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources indicate that reimbursement is based on the 'acquisition cost' or 'actual acquisition cost' of the 340B drug [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources require the use of specific claim level identifiers when submitting 340B drug claims [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly details the reimbursement amount as the '340B Acquisition Cost' plus a specific professional dispensing fee of $10.18 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01], whereas policy documents primarily state the requirement to submit at 'actual acquisition cost' without detailing the full reimbursement calculation [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01].

Policy documents mention the 'UD modifier' for FFS-only claims (effective July 1, 2022) [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01], while the provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, specifies particular NCPDP fields for claim level identifiers, such as Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 and Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that applying claim level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims is considered fraudulent billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01], a specific warning not found in the provided policy excerpts.

The provider manual explicitly references the federal law prohibiting duplicate discounts and the role of the federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01], while policy documents focus on the covered entity's responsibility to prevent duplicate rebates [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:14 — 2021-11-01].

Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field Medicaid Primary Claim (Medicare. Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 N/A
409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost N/A
Lowest Net Charge to Cash Lowest Net Charge to Cash
426-DQ Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) Customers Customers
FAQs on the 340B program itself, as well as information on how to ask additional questions, can be found on the HRSA
website at: https://www.hrsa.gov/opa/faqs/index.html.

These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.

test_id_48.ground_truth:
"Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.

drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity."
