test_id_49.question: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: The New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual (effective 2025-10-01) is more recent than the newest policy update (effective 2023-06-01) and should receive more weight. Both sources indicate that 340B drugs are reimbursed based on their acquisition cost, requiring proper identification and National Drug Code (NDC) reporting to prevent duplicate discounts.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source, New York State Medicaid Update June 2023, all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims, when NYS Medicaid is the primary payor, must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01]. This requirement applies to claims billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) Plans, or as secondary claims, whether medical or pharmacy [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01]. Previously, for NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims, drugs obtained via the 340B program had to be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01]. Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all billed drugs, and if the NDC is not referenced, NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01]. Covered entities are responsible for correctly identifying 340B claims to ensure rebates are not collected for these drugs, and if rebates are received due to incorrect or missing identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the 340B discounts [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:14 — 2021-11-01]. Claims submitted with incorrect data, including cost in the wrong field, may be denied [New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6:4, 5 — 2019-05-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source, New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, 340B-purchased drugs are reimbursed at their "340B Acquisition Cost" regardless of NADAC availability, and include a professional dispensing fee of "$10.18" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01]. The pharmacy is reimbursed for medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using the established reimbursement methodology [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01]. Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts, meaning manufacturers are not required to provide both a discounted 340B price and a Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01]. Covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for Medicaid patients and adhere to federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) rules and requirements, including listing the entity [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources indicate that 340B drugs are reimbursed based on their acquisition cost: "actual acquisition cost" [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01] and "340B Acquisition Cost" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources require the reporting of the National Drug Code (NDC) for reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources acknowledge the prohibition of duplicate discounts, requiring proper identification of 340B claims to prevent manufacturers from providing both a 340B discount and a Medicaid drug rebate [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:14 — 2021-11-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states a professional dispensing fee of "$10.18" for 340B-purchased drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01], which is not mentioned in the provided policy documents regarding 340B reimbursement.

The newest policy document (June 2023) specifies that 340B claims must be submitted with claim level identifiers for a broader scope, including NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) Plan, or secondary claims, whether medical or pharmacy [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01], while an older policy (July 2022) specifically mentioned appending the "UD modifier" for "NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01]. The provider manual mentions HRSA rules for listing the entity but does not detail specific claim-level identifiers like the UD modifier or the broader scope of claims.

The provider manual explicitly states that 340B acquisition cost is used "If NADAC is available" and "If NADAC is unavailable" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01], whereas policy documents generally refer to "actual acquisition cost" without detailing the NADAC availability condition.
comparison: Both sources indicate that 340B drugs are reimbursed based on their acquisition cost: "actual acquisition cost" [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01] and "340B Acquisition Cost" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources require the reporting of the National Drug Code (NDC) for reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources acknowledge the prohibition of duplicate discounts, requiring proper identification of 340B claims to prevent manufacturers from providing both a 340B discount and a Medicaid drug rebate [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:14 — 2021-11-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly states a professional dispensing fee of "$10.18" for 340B-purchased drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01], which is not mentioned in the provided policy documents regarding 340B reimbursement.

The newest policy document (June 2023) specifies that 340B claims must be submitted with claim level identifiers for a broader scope, including NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) Plan, or secondary claims, whether medical or pharmacy [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01], while an older policy (July 2022) specifically mentioned appending the "UD modifier" for "NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01]. The provider manual mentions HRSA rules for listing the entity but does not detail specific claim-level identifiers like the UD modifier or the broader scope of claims.

The provider manual explicitly states that 340B acquisition cost is used "If NADAC is available" and "If NADAC is unavailable" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01], whereas policy documents generally refer to "actual acquisition cost" without detailing the NADAC availability condition.
answers.caveats: The policy documents provide more specific details regarding claim submission requirements and the consequences of incorrect identification for preventing duplicate rebates, particularly for different claim types (FFS, MMC, primary/secondary). The provider manual, being more recent, offers a clear reimbursement methodology including a specific dispensing fee. Given the provider manual's later effective date, its guidance on reimbursement methodology and dispensing fee should be considered the most current.

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
It is the responsibility of the covered entity
(CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with the 340B stock
of the CE for 340B-eligible Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for these drugs. If rebates
are received by the NYS Department of Health (DOH) for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims. Please note: 340B claims billed to Medicaid FFS as secondary claims also
require identifiers, as well as 340B claims billed to MMC Plans.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The information in this article is intended to advise
providers, who may be new to submitting claims to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, that
all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor must be
submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost.
Claim level identifiers are required for any 340B-purchased drug billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care
(MMC) Plan, or as a secondary claim, whether it is a medical or pharmacy claim. It is the responsibility of the
covered entity (CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with
the 340B stock of the CE for 340B-eligible NYS Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for
these drugs. If rebates are received by the NYS DOH for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
4

Pharmacy
Reminder: 340B Providers, Covered Entities, and Contract Pharmacies
A review of 340B claims submitted via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format
indicates confusion as to which field should contain the ingredient cost and which should contain the Usual and
Customary (U&C). 340B drug claims need to be properly identified as 340B as well as submitted at costs in the
appropriate fields. Claims submitted with incorrect data, including cost in the wrong field, are subject to
audit and recovery.
The following fields are required on each Medicaid 340B drug claim submitted NCPDP format:
Medicaid Primary Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field
Claim (Primary: Medicare; Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 Not applicable
409-D9, Ingredient Cost Submitted * 340B Acquisition
Not applicable
Cost
426-DQ, Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) * Lowest Net Charge Lowest Net Charge to Cash
to Cash Customers Customers
*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pricing methodology is systematically determined as follows:
Drug Type If NADAC is available If NADAC is unavailable, Professional Dispensing Fee*
reimburse at: reimburse at:
Lower of NADAC, FUL, Lower of WAC – 17.5%,
Generics SMAC or U&C FUL, SMAC, or U&C $10.18
Lower of NADAC or
Brands U&C Lower of WAC or U&C $10.18
Lower of NADAC, FUL,
OTCs SMAC or U&C Lower of WAC, FUL, $10.18
SMAC, or U&C
340B**Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost 340B Acquisition Cost $10.18
Drug
Note: Claims will pay at the pharmacy's Usual and Customary (U&C) pricing if lower than drug ingredient cost plus
dispensing fee.
* Professional Dispensing Fee applies if the drug meets the definition of Covered Outpatient Drug and is not paid at
U&C.
**When claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim. See subsection 340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
below for more information.
Federal Upper Limit (FUL) is determined by the Secretary of Health and Human Services, a price ceiling used by Centers
for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) to control prices for certain medications paid to pharmacies.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual (effective October 1, 2025) is more recent than the newest policy document (effective June 1, 2023) and should receive more weight by default. According to the provider manual, 340B drugs are reimbursed at their acquisition cost plus a professional dispensing fee, while policy documents also emphasize submission at actual acquisition cost and require specific claim identifiers.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy document, 340B-purchased drug claims, when NYS Medicaid is the primary payor, must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01]. Claim level identifiers are required for any 340B-purchased drug billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) Plan, or as a secondary claim, whether it is a medical or pharmacy claim [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01]. For NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims, drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01]. Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all billed drugs, and if the NDC is not referenced, NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01]. Covered entities are responsible for correctly identifying claims to ensure rebates are not collected for 340B drugs, and incorrect identification may lead to the CE reimbursing manufacturers for 340B discounts [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:14 — 2021-11-01]. Claims submitted with incorrect data, including cost in the wrong field, may be denied [New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6:4, 5 — 2019-05-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, 340B purchased drugs are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost, regardless of whether NADAC is available, plus a Professional Dispensing Fee of $10.18 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01]. All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:30, 31 — 2025-10-01]. Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts, meaning manufacturers are not required to provide both a discounted 340B price and a Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01]. Covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for their Medicaid patients and adhere to federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) rules and requirements, including listing the entity [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01]. Reimbursement for the medication is provided when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using the reimbursement methodology [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources indicate that 340B drugs are reimbursed based on their acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources require the use of National Drug Code (NDC) for reimbursement, stating that claims without an NDC will not be reimbursed or that reimbursement is provided when billed via NDC [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources emphasize the importance of correctly identifying 340B drugs on claims to prevent duplicate discounts or rebates [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:14 — 2021-11-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly details the reimbursement calculation for 340B drugs as the "340B Acquisition Cost" plus a fixed "$10.18" Professional Dispensing Fee [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents state claims must be submitted "at actual acquisition cost" [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01] but do not specify a dispensing fee or the exact calculation method.

Policy documents provide specific claim identifiers, such as appending the "UD modifier" for NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01] and requiring "claim level identifiers" for NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor, or for NYRx, MMC, or secondary claims [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01]. The provided provider manual chunks mention the need for correct identification but do not detail specific modifiers or claim level identifiers for submission.

The provider manual explicitly states that "All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:30, 31 — 2025-10-01]. While policy documents refer to "NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01], they do not make a general statement about all prescription drugs being reimbursed on a FFS basis.
comparison: Both sources indicate that 340B drugs are reimbursed based on their acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources require the use of National Drug Code (NDC) for reimbursement, stating that claims without an NDC will not be reimbursed or that reimbursement is provided when billed via NDC [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources emphasize the importance of correctly identifying 340B drugs on claims to prevent duplicate discounts or rebates [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:14 — 2021-11-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly details the reimbursement calculation for 340B drugs as the "340B Acquisition Cost" plus a fixed "$10.18" Professional Dispensing Fee [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents state claims must be submitted "at actual acquisition cost" [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01] but do not specify a dispensing fee or the exact calculation method.

Policy documents provide specific claim identifiers, such as appending the "UD modifier" for NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01] and requiring "claim level identifiers" for NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor, or for NYRx, MMC, or secondary claims [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01]. The provided provider manual chunks mention the need for correct identification but do not detail specific modifiers or claim level identifiers for submission.

The provider manual explicitly states that "All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:30, 31 — 2025-10-01]. While policy documents refer to "NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01], they do not make a general statement about all prescription drugs being reimbursed on a FFS basis.
answers.caveats: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is significantly more recent than the newest policy document (June 1, 2023) and therefore should be given more weight for current guidance. While the provider manual offers a clear reimbursement formula, the policy documents provide more granular details regarding specific claim identifiers (e.g., UD modifier) that are not explicitly present in the provided provider manual excerpts.

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
It is the responsibility of the covered entity
(CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with the 340B stock
of the CE for 340B-eligible Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for these drugs. If rebates
are received by the NYS Department of Health (DOH) for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims. Please note: 340B claims billed to Medicaid FFS as secondary claims also
require identifiers, as well as 340B claims billed to MMC Plans.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The information in this article is intended to advise
providers, who may be new to submitting claims to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, that
all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor must be
submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost.
Claim level identifiers are required for any 340B-purchased drug billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care
(MMC) Plan, or as a secondary claim, whether it is a medical or pharmacy claim. It is the responsibility of the
covered entity (CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with
the 340B stock of the CE for 340B-eligible NYS Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for
these drugs. If rebates are received by the NYS DOH for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
4

Pharmacy
Reminder: 340B Providers, Covered Entities, and Contract Pharmacies
A review of 340B claims submitted via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format
indicates confusion as to which field should contain the ingredient cost and which should contain the Usual and
Customary (U&C). 340B drug claims need to be properly identified as 340B as well as submitted at costs in the
appropriate fields. Claims submitted with incorrect data, including cost in the wrong field, are subject to
audit and recovery.
The following fields are required on each Medicaid 340B drug claim submitted NCPDP format:
Medicaid Primary Medicaid Secondary Claim

Not applicable

to Cash Customers Customers
*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pricing methodology is systematically determined as follows:
Drug Type If NADAC is available If NADAC is unavailable, Professional Dispensing Fee*
reimburse at: reimburse at:
Lower of NADAC, FUL, Lower of WAC – 17.5%,
Generics SMAC or U&C FUL, SMAC, or U&C $10.18
Lower of NADAC or
Brands U&C Lower of WAC or U&C $10.18
Lower of NADAC, FUL,
OTCs SMAC or U&C Lower of WAC, FUL, $10.18
SMAC, or U&C
340B**Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost 340B Acquisition Cost $10.18
Drug
Note: Claims will pay at the pharmacy's Usual and Customary (U&C) pricing if lower than drug ingredient cost plus
dispensing fee.
* Professional Dispensing Fee applies if the drug meets the definition of Covered Outpatient Drug and is not paid at
U&C.
**When claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim. See subsection 340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
below for more information.
Federal Upper Limit (FUL) is determined by the Secretary of Health and Human Services, a price ceiling used by Centers
for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) to control prices for certain medications paid to pharmacies.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_50.ground_truth:
"Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.

drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity."
test_id_50.pm_ground_truth: Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.
test_id_50.policy_ground_truth: drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity.

test_id_51.question: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_51.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides direct guidance on home infusion drugs, stating they are not covered by a bundled payment and must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit. This is more recent and specific than the newest policy documents, effective July 1, 2022, which discuss coverage for administered drugs generally but do not specifically define or detail coverage for 'home infusion drugs' or their unique billing structure.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source dated July 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid FFS policy covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where the medically accepted indication is supported in compendia described in Social Security Act §1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01]. In the absence of such an indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01]. An older policy document from July 1, 2020, states it is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, such as the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, the New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies, and services associated with home infusion treatments [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01]. Drugs are evaluated for pharmacy benefit coverage if they can be billed in the NCPDP format and dispensed by a pharmacy [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources acknowledge the coverage of drugs administered via methods relevant to infusion (subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods) [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources imply that pharmacies have a role in billing for drugs, either generally or specifically for home infusion drugs under the pharmacy benefit [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly defines and addresses 'Home Infusion' drugs and their specific billing mechanisms, stating there is no bundled payment and they must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. The policy documents discuss coverage for administered drugs generally, often in practitioner's office settings, without specifically defining 'home infusion drugs' or their unique billing structure [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that NYS Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion drugs, supplies, and services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. The policy documents do not address the concept of bundled payments for home infusion.

The provider manual specifies criteria for home infusion drugs to be covered under the pharmacy benefit, such as being billable in NCPDP format and able to be dispensed by a pharmacy [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01]. The policy documents do not provide such specific criteria for home infusion drugs.
The newest provider manual is effective October 1, 2025, making it significantly more recent than the newest policy document, which is effective July 1, 2022.
comparison: Both sources acknowledge the coverage of drugs administered via methods relevant to infusion (subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods) [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources imply that pharmacies have a role in billing for drugs, either generally or specifically for home infusion drugs under the pharmacy benefit [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly defines and addresses 'Home Infusion' drugs and their specific billing mechanisms, stating there is no bundled payment and they must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. The policy documents discuss coverage for administered drugs generally, often in practitioner's office settings, without specifically defining 'home infusion drugs' or their unique billing structure [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that NYS Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion drugs, supplies, and services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. The policy documents do not address the concept of bundled payments for home infusion.

The provider manual specifies criteria for home infusion drugs to be covered under the pharmacy benefit, such as being billable in NCPDP format and able to be dispensed by a pharmacy [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01]. The policy documents do not provide such specific criteria for home infusion drugs.
The newest provider manual is effective October 1, 2025, making it significantly more recent than the newest policy document, which is effective July 1, 2022.
answers.caveats: The policy documents do not specifically define 'home infusion drugs' or their unique billing structure, focusing more on drugs administered in clinical settings. While the provider manual clarifies that home infusion drugs are billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit, it does not explicitly state *who* (e.g., the home health agency, the dispensing pharmacy, or the administering practitioner) is ultimately responsible for billing the drug itself when administered in the home setting, beyond implying the appropriate provider for the chosen benefit.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
FFS Billing:
• Payment for the drug’s administration will be made through the outpatient Ambulatory Patient Groups
(APG) payment when administered in a clinic setting or, if administered on an inpatient basis, the All
Patient Refined Diagnosis Related Groups (APR-DRG) payment.
• Facilities administering ZOLGENSMA® will be reimbursed for the cost of the drug using the ordered
ambulatory fee schedule. The ordered ambulatory claim should be submitted on paper (using the
eMedNY 150003 claim form) and should include the facility’s actual acquisition cost by invoice.
Documentation of medical necessity that includes the criteria listed above must accompany the
claim. Ordered ambulatory billing guidelines can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
OrderedAmbulatory/PDFS/OrderedAmbulatory_Billing_Guidelines.pdf
• The following documentation must be included with the claim:
Manufacturer’s invoice showing the acquisition cost of the biologic, including all discounts, rebates
or incentives;
Documentation of the medication administration; and
Documentation of the criteria listed under NYS FFS Coverage Policy.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.

test_id_52.question: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_52.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

answers.headline_summary: The Provider Manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is significantly more recent than the newest relevant Policy document (July 1, 2020, for billing responsibility, or October 1, 2022, for general pharmacy billing reminders) and should receive more weight for current guidance. According to the Provider Manual, home infusion drugs are covered as a pharmacy or medical benefit, not a bundled payment, and the provider/pharmacy is responsible for determining other insurance coverage and billing the appropriate party, including third-party insurers first.
answers.policy_definition:
Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define how home infusion drugs are specifically covered for Policy. The policy documents discuss payment for drug administration in clinic/inpatient settings [New York State Medicaid Update November 2019 Volume 35 Number 11:5 — 2019-11-01] and general pharmacy billing reminders [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:7 — 2022-10-01], but do not define the coverage mechanism for home infusion drugs themselves.

Regarding billing responsibility, it is the "pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01]. Providers, including pharmacies, are responsible for actively and regularly seeking Third Party Liability (TPL) enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01]. New York State Medicaid is always the payor of last resort, requiring providers to obtain correct billing information, bill third-party insurers first (including commercial plans or Medicare), and resolve any claim issues, such as prior authorization, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid as secondary [New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7:5 — 2019-06-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition:
The New York State Medicaid Program "does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. Instead, "Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for "certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01]. Drugs are evaluated for pharmacy benefit coverage based on criteria such as being billable in NCPDP format and able to be dispensed by a pharmacy [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01]. The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part D [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].

Regarding billing responsibility, it is the "responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23]. If the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare, the amount paid by the other insurance carrier must be entered in the "Other Insurance Paid" field [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23].
similarities: Both sources indicate that providers/pharmacies are responsible for determining if a patient has other insurance coverage and billing the appropriate party, including third-party insurers, before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7:5 — 2019-06-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23].

Both imply that Medicaid acts as a secondary payer when other insurance is available, requiring other insurance to be billed first [New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7:5 — 2019-06-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23].
differences: The Provider Manual explicitly defines how home infusion drugs are covered, stating they are not a bundled payment but must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], whereas the Policy documents do not provide a specific definition for home infusion drug coverage [Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for Policy].

The Provider Manual specifies that home infusion drugs can be covered under both the medical and pharmacy benefits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01], a detail not present in the provided Policy excerpts.

The Provider Manual mentions CMS requirements for Medicare Part D coverage of home infusion drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], which is not discussed in the provided Policy documents.
comparison: Both sources indicate that providers/pharmacies are responsible for determining if a patient has other insurance coverage and billing the appropriate party, including third-party insurers, before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7:5 — 2019-06-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23].

Both imply that Medicaid acts as a secondary payer when other insurance is available, requiring other insurance to be billed first [New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7:5 — 2019-06-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23].

The Provider Manual explicitly defines how home infusion drugs are covered, stating they are not a bundled payment but must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], whereas the Policy documents do not provide a specific definition for home infusion drug coverage [Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for Policy].

The Provider Manual specifies that home infusion drugs can be covered under both the medical and pharmacy benefits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01], a detail not present in the provided Policy excerpts.

The Provider Manual mentions CMS requirements for Medicare Part D coverage of home infusion drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], which is not discussed in the provided Policy documents.
answers.caveats: None

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.

policy.text:
This could
involve prior authorization (PA) requirements, appeal processes, or changes to medications ordered
to align with the primary plan formulary products, etc. Providers should refer to the Reminder: Pharmacy
Billing for Medicaid Patients with other Coverage article published in the November 2021 issue of the Medicaid
Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2021/docs/mu_no13_
nov21_pr.pdf, for more information regarding this topic. If all attempts for coverage have been exhausted and
coverage has not been granted from the primary insurer, NYS DOH may consider the denial under special
circumstances. The medication in question would still be subject to any editing requirements under the NYS
Medicaid FFS program. Submitting Third Party Liability (TPL) -covered claims that incorrectly bypass the TPL
Plan responsibility of payment is considered inaccurate billing and may be subject to audit recoveries.
Questions and Additional Information:
• All questions regarding this update should be directed to the Medicaid Pharmacy Policy Unit by
telephone at (518) 486-3209 or by email at NYRx@health.ny.gov.

policy.text:
FFS Billing:
• Payment for the drug’s administration will be made through the outpatient Ambulatory Patient Groups
(APG) payment when administered in a clinic setting or, if administered on an inpatient basis, the All
Patient Refined Diagnosis Related Groups (APR-DRG) payment.
• Facilities administering ZOLGENSMA® will be reimbursed for the cost of the drug using the ordered
ambulatory fee schedule. The ordered ambulatory claim should be submitted on paper (using the
eMedNY 150003 claim form) and should include the facility’s actual acquisition cost by invoice.
Documentation of medical necessity that includes the criteria listed above must accompany the
claim. Ordered ambulatory billing guidelines can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
OrderedAmbulatory/PDFS/OrderedAmbulatory_Billing_Guidelines.pdf
• The following documentation must be included with the claim:
Manufacturer’s invoice showing the acquisition cost of the biologic, including all discounts, rebates
or incentives;
Documentation of the medication administration; and
Documentation of the criteria listed under NYS FFS Coverage Policy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2019 Volume 35 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
New York State Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; every effort must be taken to
obtain correct billing information.
If TPL is applicable, providers must the third-party insurer first, including commercial plans or Medicare,
and bill Medicaid as secondary, if appropriate. Any claim issues must be resolved with the third-party insurer,
including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary
insurer does not cover a specific medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the
third-party insurer will result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
If the member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs. Information on Part B covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers, or
• Medicare Part D for other prescription drugs. Information on Part D covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
If the member has Medicare Parts A or B (or both) and does not have Medicare Part D, providers will bill:
• Part D covered claims to Medicare Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET).
