test_id_53.question: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_53.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

answers.headline_summary: The Provider Manual, effective October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest Policy Update from July 1, 2022, and should receive more weight for current guidance. It clarifies that home infusion drugs are not covered by a bundled payment and must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit, with pharmacists responsible for appropriate dispensing.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy documents, NYS Medicaid FFS policy covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in a practitioner’s office for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications or where the medically accepted indication is supported in recognized compendia [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — 2019-02-01]. Payment for the drug’s administration in a clinic setting is made through the outpatient Ambulatory Patient Groups (APG) payment, or if administered on an inpatient basis, through the All Patient Refined Diagnosis Related Groups (APR-DRG) payment [New York State Medicaid Update November 2019 Volume 35 Number 11:5 — 2019-11-01]. The policy documents state it is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, such as the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01]. Both the facility and the pharmacy are responsible for actively and regularly seeking Third Party Liability (TPL) enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01]. The policy documents do not specifically define coverage for "home infusion drugs" as a distinct category.
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, the New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies, and services associated with home infusion treatments [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider to be available to members via both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit, provided they meet criteria such as being billable in NCPDP format and able to be dispensed by a pharmacy [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01]. The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part D [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. It is the responsibility of the pharmacist to ensure practitioner-administration drugs, including those for home infusion, are appropriately dispensed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:13 — 2025-10-01]. Providers can refer to the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines for correct billing guidance for these products [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:35, 36 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources acknowledge the coverage of drugs administered via subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods, though the policy documents focus on office/clinic settings and the provider manual specifically addresses home infusion [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — 2019-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources indicate a role for pharmacies in the dispensing and/or billing of drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:13 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that the NYS Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion treatments, requiring drugs and supplies to be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. The policy documents do not contain this specific information regarding home infusion bundled payments.

The provider manual specifically addresses "home infusion drugs" as a distinct category for coverage and billing, detailing that they can be covered under both medical and pharmacy benefits if certain criteria are met [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12, 19 — 2025-10-01]. The policy documents discuss coverage for generally administered drugs in office/clinic settings but do not specifically define coverage for "home infusion drugs."

The policy documents emphasize the pharmacy's responsibility for timely billing to the appropriate party (e.g., facility, commercial, Medicare, Medicaid) [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01]. The provider manual focuses on *how* home infusion drugs must be billed (pharmacy or medical benefit) and the pharmacist's responsibility for appropriate dispensing of practitioner-administered drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:13, 19 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both sources acknowledge the coverage of drugs administered via subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods, though the policy documents focus on office/clinic settings and the provider manual specifically addresses home infusion [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — 2019-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources indicate a role for pharmacies in the dispensing and/or billing of drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:13 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that the NYS Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion treatments, requiring drugs and supplies to be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. The policy documents do not contain this specific information regarding home infusion bundled payments.

The provider manual specifically addresses "home infusion drugs" as a distinct category for coverage and billing, detailing that they can be covered under both medical and pharmacy benefits if certain criteria are met [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12, 19 — 2025-10-01]. The policy documents discuss coverage for generally administered drugs in office/clinic settings but do not specifically define coverage for "home infusion drugs."

The policy documents emphasize the pharmacy's responsibility for timely billing to the appropriate party (e.g., facility, commercial, Medicare, Medicaid) [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01]. The provider manual focuses on *how* home infusion drugs must be billed (pharmacy or medical benefit) and the pharmacist's responsibility for appropriate dispensing of practitioner-administered drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:13, 19 — 2025-10-01].
answers.caveats: The policy documents primarily discuss coverage and billing for drugs administered in practitioner offices, clinics, or inpatient settings, and do not specifically define "home infusion drugs" or their unique coverage/billing mechanisms. The provider manual, being more recent and specific to "Home Infusion," provides direct guidance on this concept.

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
FFS Billing:
• Payment for the drug’s administration will be made through the outpatient Ambulatory Patient Groups
(APG) payment when administered in a clinic setting or, if administered on an inpatient basis, the All
Patient Refined Diagnosis Related Groups (APR-DRG) payment.
• Facilities administering ZOLGENSMA® will be reimbursed for the cost of the drug using the ordered
ambulatory fee schedule. The ordered ambulatory claim should be submitted on paper (using the
eMedNY 150003 claim form) and should include the facility’s actual acquisition cost by invoice.
Documentation of medical necessity that includes the criteria listed above must accompany the
claim. Ordered ambulatory billing guidelines can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
OrderedAmbulatory/PDFS/OrderedAmbulatory_Billing_Guidelines.pdf
• The following documentation must be included with the claim:
Manufacturer’s invoice showing the acquisition cost of the biologic, including all discounts, rebates
or incentives;
Documentation of the medication administration; and
Documentation of the criteria listed under NYS FFS Coverage Policy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2019 Volume 35 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prescribers should use their professional judgement to determine the best method for members to obtain practitioner
administration drugs. It is the responsibility of the pharmacist to ensure white or brown bagged drugs are
appropriately dispensed.
This policy refers to any drug being dispensed by a pharmacy for practitioner-administration to a NYRx member,
including those billed as a secondary payment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_54.question: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_54.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

answers.headline_summary: The newest Provider Manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides specific guidance on home infusion drug coverage and billing, stating there is no bundled payment and drugs must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit. In contrast, the newest Policy document, effective July 1, 2022, discusses general coverage for drugs administered by various methods in a practitioner's office but does not specifically address 'home infusion drugs' or their unique billing structure. The Provider Manual is more recent and directly addresses the target concept, and therefore should receive more weight.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy document, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where the medically accepted indication is supported in compendia described in Social Security Act §1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01]. In the absence of such an indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01]. Older policy also states that drugs administered by these methods are covered for FDA-approved indications and recognized off-label indications listed in drug compendia [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — 2019-02-01]. Payment for drug administration in a clinic setting is made through outpatient Ambulatory Patient Groups (APG) payment, or if inpatient, through All Patient Refined Diagnosis Related Groups (APR-DRG) payment [New York State Medicaid Update November 2019 Volume 35 Number 11:5 — 2019-11-01]. It is the responsibility of providers, including pharmacies, to actively seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility and for the pharmacy to bill timely to the appropriate party (facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid) [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01]. However, the policy documents do not specifically define coverage or billing responsibilities for 'home infusion drugs'.
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, the New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies, and services associated with home infusion treatments [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. NYS Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider, need to be available to members via both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01]. For a drug to be eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit, it must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format and be billable in units [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01]. Providers can refer to the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines for correct billing guidance for these products [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:35, 36 — 2025-10-01]. It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and whether that insurance covers the service being billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23].
similarities: Both sources acknowledge coverage for drugs administered via methods relevant to infusion (subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous) [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources indicate a general provider responsibility for determining other insurance coverage and billing appropriately [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23].
differences: The Provider Manual explicitly defines the coverage model for 'Home Infusion' drugs, stating that the NYS Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion treatments, and that drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents do not provide this specific definition or coverage model for 'home infusion drugs'.

The Provider Manual specifies criteria for home infusion drugs to be eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit, such as being billable in NCPDP format and in units [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents do not detail such specific criteria for home infusion drugs.

The Provider Manual directly addresses 'home infusion drugs' and their availability through both medical and pharmacy benefits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents discuss general drug administration coverage in clinic/office settings but do not specifically mention 'home infusion drugs' as a distinct category with specific billing instructions.
comparison: Both sources acknowledge coverage for drugs administered via methods relevant to infusion (subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous) [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources indicate a general provider responsibility for determining other insurance coverage and billing appropriately [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23].

The Provider Manual explicitly defines the coverage model for 'Home Infusion' drugs, stating that the NYS Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion treatments, and that drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents do not provide this specific definition or coverage model for 'home infusion drugs'.

The Provider Manual specifies criteria for home infusion drugs to be eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit, such as being billable in NCPDP format and in units [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents do not detail such specific criteria for home infusion drugs.

The Provider Manual directly addresses 'home infusion drugs' and their availability through both medical and pharmacy benefits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents discuss general drug administration coverage in clinic/office settings but do not specifically mention 'home infusion drugs' as a distinct category with specific billing instructions.
answers.caveats: None

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.

test_id_54.ground_truth:
The drug must be dispensed by a licensed pharmacy. It implies that the pharmacy is responsible for billing for the drug product itself.
The updates implicitly solidify the rule that a pharmacy cannot bill for the professional nursing component.

test_id_55.question: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_55.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

answers.headline_summary: The New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, effective October 1, 2025, is significantly more recent and directly addresses home infusion drugs, stating that NYS Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment and requires billing as a pharmacy or medical benefit, with specific instructions for pharmacies. The policy documents, with the newest relevant effective date of July 1, 2022, provide general guidance on drug administration coverage and billing but do not specifically define coverage or billing for 'home infusion drugs' as a distinct category.
answers.policy_definition:
Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define 'home infusion drugs' coverage or specific billing responsibility for them directly within the policy documents. However, the policy documents provide general guidance on drug administration and billing:

NYS Medicaid FFS policy covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in a practitioner’s office for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where the medically accepted indication is supported in compendia described in Social Security Act §1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01].

Payment for drug administration in a clinic setting is made through the outpatient Ambulatory Patient Groups (APG) payment, or if administered on an inpatient basis, through the All Patient Refined Diagnosis Related Groups (APR-DRG) payment [New York State Medicaid Update November 2019 Volume 35 Number 11:5 — 2019-11-01].

It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, such as the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01]. Paper claim submission for multiple drug procedure codes, including wastage, must be submitted for the same date of service on separate claim forms [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:6 — 2022-07-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition:
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies, and services associated with home infusion treatments [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].

NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider to be available to members via both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01]. Drugs are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit if they can be billed in the NCPDP format and dispensed by a pharmacy [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].

For billing, if both the drug and administration are being billed, these claims must be submitted separately [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01]. For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field, and the pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01]. For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01]. Pharmacies must keep these orders on file [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources indicate that drugs requiring administration by a practitioner are covered under Medicaid, although the policy documents do not specifically mention 'home infusion drugs' in this context [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources imply that pharmacies have a role in billing for drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources suggest that claims for drugs and their administration may need separate submission or distinct handling [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:6 — 2022-07-01] (for multiple drug procedure codes) and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01] (for drug and administration).
differences: The provider manual explicitly defines 'Home Infusion' coverage and billing, stating that NYS Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment and requires billing as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. The policy documents do not specifically address 'home infusion drugs' coverage or billing.

The provider manual specifies that pharmacies are responsible for billing home infusion drugs (via NDC using the ordering prescriber’s NPI) and their administration (using procedure code 96372 with the pharmacy’s NPI) separately [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents mention general pharmacy billing responsibility but lack specific details for home infusion.

The provider manual has a significantly more recent effective date (2025-10-01) and directly addresses the target concept, making it the primary source for current guidance. The policy documents' newest relevant date is 2022-07-01, and they provide more general guidance on drug administration and billing.

The provider manual explicitly defines 'Home Infusion' coverage and billing, stating that NYS Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment and requires billing as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. The policy documents do not specifically address 'home infusion drugs' coverage or billing.

The provider manual specifies that pharmacies are responsible for billing home infusion drugs (via NDC using the ordering prescriber’s NPI) and their administration (using procedure code 96372 with the pharmacy’s NPI) separately [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents mention general pharmacy billing responsibility but lack specific details for home infusion.

The provider manual has a significantly more recent effective date (2025-10-01) and directly addresses the target concept, making it the primary source for current guidance. The policy documents' newest relevant date is 2022-07-01, and they provide more general guidance on drug administration and billing.

policy.text:
FFS Billing:
• Payment for the drug’s administration will be made through the outpatient Ambulatory Patient Groups
(APG) payment when administered in a clinic setting or, if administered on an inpatient basis, the All
Patient Refined Diagnosis Related Groups (APR-DRG) payment.
• Facilities administering ZOLGENSMA® will be reimbursed for the cost of the drug using the ordered
ambulatory fee schedule. The ordered ambulatory claim should be submitted on paper (using the
eMedNY 150003 claim form) and should include the facility’s actual acquisition cost by invoice.
Documentation of medical necessity that includes the criteria listed above must accompany the
claim. Ordered ambulatory billing guidelines can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
OrderedAmbulatory/PDFS/OrderedAmbulatory_Billing_Guidelines.pdf
• The following documentation must be included with the claim:
Manufacturer’s invoice showing the acquisition cost of the biologic, including all discounts, rebates
or incentives;
Documentation of the medication administration; and
Documentation of the criteria listed under NYS FFS Coverage Policy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2019 Volume 35 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Paper claim submission and multiple drug procedure codes, including wastage, must be submitted for the
same date of service on separate claim forms. Additional information on paper claim submission can be found
in the Billing Instructions for Physician-Administered Drugs (J-codes) Submitted on Paper Claims article,
published in the December 2008 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2008/2008-12.htm#ins.
Practitioner Billing
Reimbursement for drugs, including vaccines and immune globulins, furnished by practitioners to their
patients, is based on the acquisition cost to the practitioner. For all items furnished in this fashion, practitioners
are expected to maintain auditable records of the actual itemized invoice cost of the drug. This includes the
numbers of doses of the drug represented on the invoice. NYS Medicaid FFS does not intend to pay more
than the actual acquisition cost of the drug dosage, as established by invoice, to the practitioner.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_56.question: How is naloxone reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides the most recent and detailed guidance on naloxone reimbursement, specifying that the medication is reimbursed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology and administration via procedure code 96372. Policy documents, most recently updated February 1, 2024, confirm coverage for over-the-counter (OTC) naloxone when prescribed or dispensed under a standing order, with the provider manual receiving more weight due to its later effective date.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source dated February 1, 2024, New York State (NYS) Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed by the practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4, 5 — 2024-02-01]. This expanded coverage for OTC naloxone was also noted in September 2023 [New York State Medicaid Update September 2023 Volume 39 Number 14:7, 8 — 2023-09-01]. Earlier policy from February 2019 generally states that reimbursement will be made in accordance with existing Medicaid policy related to supervision and billing rules and requirements [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Special Edition Volume 35 Number 2:6 — 2019-02-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, the pharmacy will be reimbursed for the naloxone medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01]. If billing for both the drug and its administration, these claims must be submitted separately [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01]. For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01]. All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:30, 31 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources indicate that naloxone is covered by NYS Medicaid. The policy documents explicitly state coverage for OTC naloxone when prescribed or dispensed via standing order [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4, 5 — 2024-02-01], and the provider manual details the reimbursement mechanism for the medication itself [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides specific details on the reimbursement mechanism, stating that the medication is reimbursed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology and its administration is billed separately using procedure code 96372 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents, most recently updated February 1, 2024, focus on the conditions under which naloxone (specifically OTC) is covered (i.e., prescribed by a practitioner or dispensed via a State-issued standing order) rather than the specific billing codes or methodologies [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4, 5 — 2024-02-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that all prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:30, 31 — 2025-10-01], a detail not present in the provided policy excerpts regarding naloxone.
comparison: Both sources indicate that naloxone is covered by NYS Medicaid. Policy documents, most recently updated February 1, 2024, focus on the conditions under which naloxone (specifically OTC) is covered (i.e., prescribed by a practitioner or dispensed via a State-issued standing order) rather than the specific billing codes or methodologies [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4, 5 — 2024-02-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that all prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:30, 31 — 2025-10-01], a detail not present in the provided policy excerpts regarding naloxone.
answers.caveats: The policy documents primarily address the *conditions for coverage* of naloxone, particularly OTC naloxone, while the provider manual focuses on the *mechanisms of reimbursement* (e.g., NDC, procedure codes, fee-for-service). Therefore, they provide complementary information rather than conflicting definitions of reimbursement.

practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the
State-issued naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found on

https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention/pharmacy_standing_order.ht
m#:~:text=All%20pharmacies%20in%20New%20York,and%20opioid%2Dbased%20pain%20killers. A

• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.

These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.

pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.

test_id_56.ground_truth:
"The manual establishes naloxone as a covered drug on the Medicaid formulary, recognizing its use as an opioid antagonist. The guidance is based on a traditional prescription for a specific patient.There are standard co-payment rules for drugs. Under these general rules, a co-payment would apply unless the member is exempt.

The updates dramatically expand this policy beyond individual patients. They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used."

test_id_57.question: How is naloxone reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 2025, indicates that prescription drugs, including naloxone when prescribed, are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis, which should receive more weight by default as it is more recent than the newest policy updates from February 2024 that specify the conditions under which naloxone is covered by NYS Medicaid.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source, New York State (NYS) Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed by the practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4, 5 — 2024-02-01]. Reimbursement will be made in accordance with existing Medicaid policy related to supervision and billing rules and requirements [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Special Edition Volume 35 Number 2:6 — 2019-02-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source, all prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:30, 31 — 2025-10-01]. For residential health care facilities, prescription drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis, while non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies must be reimbursed by the facility [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:30, 31 — 2025-10-01]. Reimbursement for multiple source drugs is only available for those contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:33 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources imply that naloxone, when meeting coverage criteria, is eligible for reimbursement under NYS Medicaid.

Both sources refer to general Medicaid reimbursement policies or rules, with the policy documents stating reimbursement is 'in accordance with existing Medicaid policy related to supervision and billing rules and requirements' [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Special Edition Volume 35 Number 2:6 — 2019-02-01] and the provider manual detailing fee-for-service as the general mechanism for prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:30, 31 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The policy documents, with the newest relevant update from February 2024, specifically detail the *conditions for coverage* of naloxone, stating it is covered when prescribed or dispensed via a State-issued standing order for OTC naloxone [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4, 5 — 2024-02-01].

The provider manual documents, with the newest relevant update from October 2025, describe the general *mechanism of reimbursement* for prescription drugs as fee-for-service [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:30, 31 — 2025-10-01], without specific mention of naloxone's unique OTC coverage conditions.
comparison: Both sources imply that naloxone, when meeting coverage criteria, is eligible for reimbursement under NYS Medicaid.

Both sources refer to general Medicaid reimbursement policies or rules, with the policy documents stating reimbursement is 'in accordance with existing Medicaid policy related to supervision and billing rules and requirements' [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Special Edition Volume 35 Number 2:6 — 2019-02-01] and the provider manual detailing fee-for-service as the general mechanism for prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:30, 31 — 2025-10-01].

The policy documents, with the newest relevant update from February 2024, specifically detail the *conditions for coverage* of naloxone, stating it is covered when prescribed or dispensed via a State-issued standing order for OTC naloxone [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4, 5 — 2024-02-01].

The provider manual documents, with the newest relevant update from October 2025, describe the general *mechanism of reimbursement* for prescription drugs as fee-for-service [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:30, 31 — 2025-10-01], without specific mention of naloxone's unique OTC coverage conditions.
answers.caveats: The provider manual provides the general reimbursement mechanism (fee-for-service) for prescription drugs, which would apply to naloxone, and is more recent (October 2025). However, the policy updates (February 2024) are crucial for understanding the specific conditions under which naloxone is covered by NYS Medicaid (e.g., OTC, prescription, standing order). Therefore, both sources provide essential, complementary information for a complete understanding of naloxone reimbursement.

comparison: Both sources state that claims for the naloxone drug and its administration must be submitted separately [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2, 3 — 2024-04-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01].

Both require the claim for the drug to be submitted using the ordering prescriber's NPI in the Prescriber ID field and reimbursed via NDC using reimbursement methodology [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2, 3 — 2024-04-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01].

Both specify that the claim for administration must use procedure code "96372" and the pharmacy's NPI in the Prescriber ID field [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2, 3 — 2024-04-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01].

The policy documents explicitly mention that NYS Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed or dispensed under a standing order [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4, 5 — 2024-02-01], which is not explicitly stated in the provided provider manual excerpts.

The provider manual states that all prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis and that dispensing pharmacies may bill directly to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:30, 31 — 2025-10-01], a general principle not detailed in the provided policy excerpts specifically for naloxone.
answers.caveats: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the latest policy update from April 1, 2024, and therefore should receive more weight as current guidance. While the policy documents mention OTC coverage for naloxone, the provided provider manual excerpts do not explicitly reiterate this, though it is likely implied by the general reimbursement rules for the drug.

policy.text:
February 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 4

Reminder: Pharmacy Coverage Includes Over the Counter Naloxone
New York State (NYS) Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed by the practitioner
of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued
naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found in the NYS
Department of Health (DOH) Non-Patient Specific Prescription for Naloxone with Pharmacy Dispensing
Protocol document, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention
/docs/naloxone_standing_order_pharmacies.pdf.
To comply with NYS laws, regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, a pharmacy must submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 2

Policy and Billing
For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the National Provider Identifier (NPI) of the ordering prescriber
in the Prescriber ID field. The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug
Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology. For the administration, the claim must be submitted using
procedure code “96372” and the pharmacy NPI in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed
the administration fee of $13.36.
Questions and Additional Information:
• Pharmacists should refer to the NYSED “Pharmacists” web page, located at: https://www.op.
nysed.gov/pharmacist, for additional information.
• NYRx claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.
• NYRx Pharmacy coverage and policy questions should be directed to the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy
Policy Unit by telephone at (518) 486-3209 or by email at NYRx@health.ny.gov.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
TCN 082609865432123 was paid on
October 1, 2008. Later, the provider became aware that the patient was covered by other insurance. The other
insurance was billed, and the provider received full payment from that payer. Medicaid must be reimbursed by
submitting a void to the previously paid claim.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_58.ground_truth:
"The manual establishes naloxone as a covered drug on the Medicaid formulary, recognizing its use as an opioid antagonist. The guidance is based on a traditional prescription for a specific patient.There are standard co-payment rules for drugs. Under these general rules, a co-payment would apply unless the member is exempt.

The updates dramatically expand this policy beyond individual patients. They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used."

Under these general rules, a co-payment would apply unless the member is exempt.

They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used.

test_id_59.question: How is naloxone reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides the most current and detailed guidance on naloxone reimbursement, specifying separate claims for the drug and its administration, which largely aligns with the newest policy update, effective April 1, 2024. The provider manual should receive more weight by default due to its more recent effective date.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source, New York State (NYS) Medicaid reimburses for naloxone (drug) and its administration. For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the National Provider Identifier (NPI) of the ordering prescriber in the Prescriber ID field, and the pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2, 3 — April 01, 2024]. For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code “96372” and the pharmacy NPI in the Prescriber ID field, and a pharmacy will be reimbursed for the administration [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2, 3 — April 01, 2024]. Earlier policy updates confirm NYS Medicaid covers naloxone over-the-counter (OTC) when either prescribed by a practitioner or dispensed in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order [New York State Medicaid Update September 2023 Volume 39 Number 14:7, 8 — September 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4, 5 — February 01, 2024].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, naloxone reimbursement requires that if billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 01, 2025]. For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field, and the pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 01, 2025]. For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372 and the pharmacy’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 01, 2025]. Generally, all prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:30, 31 — October 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources specify that for the drug claim, it must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2, 3 — April 01, 2024; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources state that the pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2, 3 — April 01, 2024; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources require the claim for administration to be submitted using procedure code “96372” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2, 3 — April 01, 2024; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources require the pharmacy NPI in the Prescriber ID field for the administration claim [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2, 3 — April 01, 2024; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that if billing for both the drug and administration, "these claims must be submitted separately" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 01, 2025], whereas the policy document implies this by detailing separate requirements for each but does not explicitly state they "must be submitted separately" in the provided chunk.

Policy documents also detail the coverage of Over-the-Counter (OTC) naloxone when prescribed or dispensed via a standing order [New York State Medicaid Update September 2023 Volume 39 Number 14:7, 8 — September 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4, 5 — February 01, 2024], which is not explicitly mentioned in the provided provider manual chunks regarding reimbursement specifics.
comparison: Both sources specify that for the drug claim, it must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2, 3 — April 01, 2024; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources state that the pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2, 3 — April 01, 2024; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources require the claim for administration to be submitted using procedure code “96372” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2, 3 — April 01, 2024; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources require the pharmacy NPI in the Prescriber ID field for the administration claim [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2, 3 — April 01, 2024; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 01, 2025].

The provider manual explicitly states that if billing for both the drug and administration, "these claims must be submitted separately" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 01, 2025], whereas the policy document implies this by detailing separate requirements for each but does not explicitly state they "must be submitted separately" in the provided chunk.

Policy documents also detail the coverage of Over-the-Counter (OTC) naloxone when prescribed or dispensed via a standing order [New York State Medicaid Update September 2023 Volume 39 Number 14:7, 8 — September 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4, 5 — February 01, 2024], which is not explicitly mentioned in the provided provider manual chunks regarding reimbursement specifics.
answers.caveats: None

policy.text:
September 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Coverage Expanded to Include Over the Counter
Naloxone
New York State (NYS) Medicaid now covers naloxone over-the-counter (OTC) when either prescribed by the
practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the
State-issued naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found on
the NYS Department of Health (DOH) “Standing Order for Naloxone in Pharmacies” web page, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention/pharmacy_standing_order.ht
m#:~:text=All%20pharmacies%20in%20New%20York,and%20opioid%2Dbased%20pain%20killers. A
pharmacy, in compliance with NYS law and regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, may submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.
