They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used.

test_id_60.question: How is naloxone reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides the most recent and detailed guidance on drug reimbursement mechanisms, including separate billing for drug and administration, which should guide current practice. While policy documents, most recently updated January 1, 2025, confirm that naloxone is covered over-the-counter under specific conditions, they lack the granular reimbursement process details found in the newer provider manual.
answers.policy_definition: New York State (NYS) Medicaid covers naloxone over-the-counter (OTC) when either prescribed by the practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order [New York State Medicaid Update September 2023 Volume 39 Number 14:7, 8 — 2023-09-01; New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4, 5 — 2024-02-01]. Reimbursement is only provided for prescription and non-prescription drugs, including non-prescription therapeutic categories specified as essential, which appear on the New York State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs (NYRx) with their 11-digit NDCs [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:11, 12 — 2025-01-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:30, 31 — 2025-10-01]. Reimbursement for multiple source drugs is only available for those contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:33 — 2025-10-01]. If billing for both the drug and its administration, these claims must be submitted separately. The claim for the drug must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field, and the pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology. The claim for the administration must be submitted using procedure code 96372 and the pharmacy’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01]. Non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies must be reimbursed by the facility if they operate an institutional pharmacy or contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:30, 31 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources indicate that reimbursement for drugs is contingent on them being on the "List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs" (or NYRx) and having an 11-digit NDC [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:11, 12 — 2025-01-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:33 — 2025-10-01].
differences: Policy documents explicitly state the conditions under which naloxone is covered, specifically as an over-the-counter (OTC) medication when prescribed or dispensed via a standing order [New York State Medicaid Update September 2023 Volume 39 Number 14:7, 8 — 2023-09-01; New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4, 5 — 2024-02-01]. The provided provider manual excerpts do not explicitly mention naloxone or its OTC coverage conditions.

The provider manual provides more detailed information on the reimbursement mechanism, specifying that prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis and outlining separate billing requirements for the drug (via NDC with prescriber NPI) and its administration (via procedure code 96372 with pharmacy NPI) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15, 30, 31 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents do not detail these specific billing procedures for naloxone or other drugs.

The provider manual specifies that non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies must be reimbursed by the facility if they operate an institutional pharmacy or contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:30, 31 — 2025-10-01]. This level of detail regarding facility reimbursement for non-prescription drugs is not present in the provided policy documents.
comparison: Both sources indicate that reimbursement for drugs is contingent on them being on the "List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs" (or NYRx) and having an 11-digit NDC [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:11, 12 — 2025-01-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:33 — 2025-10-01].

Policy documents explicitly state the conditions under which naloxone is covered, specifically as an over-the-counter (OTC) medication when prescribed or dispensed via a standing order [New York State Medicaid Update September 2023 Volume 39 Number 14:7, 8 — 2023-09-01; New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4, 5 — 2024-02-01]. The provided provider manual excerpts do not explicitly mention naloxone or its OTC coverage conditions.

The provider manual provides more detailed information on the reimbursement mechanism, specifying that prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis and outlining separate billing requirements for the drug (via NDC with prescriber NPI) and its administration (via procedure code 96372 with pharmacy NPI) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15, 30, 31 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents do not detail these specific billing procedures for naloxone or other drugs.

The provider manual specifies that non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies must be reimbursed by the facility if they operate an institutional pharmacy or contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:30, 31 — 2025-10-01]. This level of detail regarding facility reimbursement for non-prescription drugs is not present in the provided policy documents.
answers.caveats: The policy documents confirm *that* naloxone is covered OTC under specific conditions, while the provider manual provides general *how-to* reimbursement details for drugs, including separate billing for administration, without explicitly mentioning naloxone OTC. It is implied that if naloxone is covered, it would follow the general reimbursement rules outlined in the provider manual.

policy.text:
December 2022
Volume 38 | Number 14
Opioid Overdose Prevention
The New York State (NYS) Department of Health (DOH) has recently released two brochures on Naloxone,
the life-saving medication that can reverse opioid overdose, for individuals who are pregnant or who’ve
recently given birth, as well as their support person(s):
• Patient education brochure tilted Do You or Someone You Know Take Opioids?
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2022 Volume 38 Number 14
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
September 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Coverage Expanded to Include Over the Counter
Naloxone
New York State (NYS) Medicaid now covers naloxone over-the-counter (OTC) when either prescribed by the
practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the
State-issued naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found on
the NYS Department of Health (DOH) “Standing Order for Naloxone in Pharmacies” web page, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention/pharmacy_standing_order.ht
m#:~:text=All%20pharmacies%20in%20New%20York,and%20opioid%2Dbased%20pain%20killers. A
pharmacy, in compliance with NYS law and regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, may submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2023 Volume 39 Number 14
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
February 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 4

Reminder: Pharmacy Coverage Includes Over the Counter Naloxone
New York State (NYS) Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed by the practitioner
of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued
naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found in the NYS
Department of Health (DOH) Non-Patient Specific Prescription for Naloxone with Pharmacy Dispensing
Protocol document, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention
/docs/naloxone_standing_order_pharmacies.pdf.
To comply with NYS laws, regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, a pharmacy must submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Only those prescription and non-prescription drugs, which appear on the list, with their 11-digit NDCs, are
reimbursable under NYRx. The list also contains those non-prescription therapeutic categories, which the
COH has specified as essential in meeting the medical needs of NYS Medicaid members.
January 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 11

Drug Coverage Limitations
NYS Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State Department of Health
List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx (unless provided
by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid, in accordance with policy and/or state or
federal legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to Social Security Act
(SSA) §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used."
